title: Austin, Texas

infobox.value: [Travis](/wiki/Travis_County,_Texas "Travis County, Texas"), [Hays](/wiki/Hays_County,_Texas "Hays County, Texas"), [Williamson](/wiki/Williamson_County,_Texas "Williamson County, Texas")

infobox.key: • [State capital](/wiki/List_of_capitals_in_the_United_States "List of capitals in the United States")

infobox.value: [32nd](/wiki/List_of_North_American_cities_by_population "List of North American cities by population") in North America [13th](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population "List of United States cities by population") in the United States [5th](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Texas "List of municipalities in Texas") in Texas

([/ˈɔːstɪn/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-us-Austin.oga "File:En-us-Austin.oga") [*AW-stin*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"))[[8]](#cite_note-9) is the [capital city](/wiki/List_of_capitals_in_the_United_States "List of capitals in the United States") of the U.S. state of [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas"). With a population of 961,855 at the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"),[[9]](#cite_note-10) it is the [13th-most populous city](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population "List of United States cities by population") in the U.S., [fifth-most populous city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Texas_by_population "List of cities in Texas by population") in Texas, and second-most populous U.S. state capital (after [Phoenix, Arizona](/wiki/Phoenix,_Arizona "Phoenix, Arizona")), while the [Austin metro area](/wiki/Greater_Austin "Greater Austin") with an estimated 2.55 million residents is the [25th-largest metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_statistical_area "Metropolitan statistical area") in the nation. Austin is the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") and most populous city of [Travis County](/wiki/Travis_County,_Texas "Travis County, Texas"), with portions extending into [Hays](/wiki/Hays_County,_Texas "Hays County, Texas") and [Williamson](/wiki/Williamson_County,_Texas "Williamson County, Texas") counties. Incorporated on December 27, 1839,[[10]](#cite_note-11) it has been one of the fastest-growing large cities in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") since 2010.[[11]](#cite_note-12)[[12]](#cite_note-13)[[13]](#cite_note-14)

**Austin** ([/ˈɔːstɪn/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-us-Austin.oga "File:En-us-Austin.oga") [*AW-stin*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"))[[8]](#cite_note-9) is the [capital city](/wiki/List_of_capitals_in_the_United_States "List of capitals in the United States") of the U.S. state of [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas"). With a population of 961,855 at the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"),[[9]](#cite_note-10) it is the [13th-most populous city](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population "List of United States cities by population") in the U.S., [fifth-most populous city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Texas_by_population "List of cities in Texas by population") in Texas, and second-most populous U.S. state capital (after [Phoenix, Arizona](/wiki/Phoenix,_Arizona "Phoenix, Arizona")), while the [Austin metro area](/wiki/Greater_Austin "Greater Austin") with an estimated 2.55 million residents is the [25th-largest metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_statistical_area "Metropolitan statistical area") in the nation. Austin is the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") and most populous city of [Travis County](/wiki/Travis_County,_Texas "Travis County, Texas"), with portions extending into [Hays](/wiki/Hays_County,_Texas "Hays County, Texas") and [Williamson](/wiki/Williamson_County,_Texas "Williamson County, Texas") counties. Incorporated on December 27, 1839,[[10]](#cite_note-11) it has been one of the fastest-growing large cities in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") since 2010.[[11]](#cite_note-12)[[12]](#cite_note-13)[[13]](#cite_note-14)

Located in [Central Texas](/wiki/Central_Texas "Central Texas") within the greater [Texas Hill Country](/wiki/Texas_Hill_Country "Texas Hill Country"), it is home to numerous lakes, rivers, and waterways, including [Lady Bird Lake](/wiki/Lady_Bird_Lake "Lady Bird Lake") and [Lake Travis](/wiki/Lake_Travis "Lake Travis") on the [Colorado River](/wiki/Colorado_River_(Texas) "Colorado River (Texas)"), [Barton Springs](/wiki/Barton_Springs_Pool "Barton Springs Pool"), [McKinney Falls](/wiki/McKinney_Falls_State_Park "McKinney Falls State Park"), and [Lake Walter E. Long](/wiki/Lake_Walter_E._Long "Lake Walter E. Long"). Evidence of human activity in the region is estimated to date back at least 11,200 years ago, with early habitation by [Clovis peoples](/wiki/Clovis_culture "Clovis culture") and later by [American Indian](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") groups such as the [Tonkawa](/wiki/Tonkawa "Tonkawa"). Austin and [San Antonio](/wiki/San_Antonio "San Antonio") are approximately 80 miles (129 km) apart, and both fall along the [I-35](/wiki/Interstate_35_in_Texas "Interstate 35 in Texas") corridor. This combined metropolitan region of [San Antonio–Austin](/wiki/San_Antonio%E2%80%93Austin_metroplex "San Antonio–Austin metroplex") has approximately 5 million people.[[14]](#cite_note-sa-atxmetro-15) Austin is the southernmost state capital in the [contiguous United States](/wiki/Contiguous_United_States "Contiguous United States") and is considered a [Gamma + level](/wiki/Globalization_and_World_Cities_Research_Network#Gamma_+ "Globalization and World Cities Research Network") [global city](/wiki/Global_city "Global city") as categorized by the [Globalization and World Cities Research Network](/wiki/Globalization_and_World_Cities_Research_Network "Globalization and World Cities Research Network").[[15]](#cite_note-16)

[Residents of Austin](/wiki/List_of_people_from_Austin,_Texas "List of people from Austin, Texas") are known as Austinites.[[16]](#cite_note-Long_2010-17) They include a diverse mix of government employees, college students, musicians, high-tech workers, and [blue-collar workers](/wiki/Blue-collar_worker "Blue-collar worker"). The city's official slogan promotes Austin as "The Live Music Capital of the World", a reference to the city's many musicians and live music venues, as well as the long-running [PBS](/wiki/PBS "PBS") TV concert series *[Austin City Limits](/wiki/Austin_City_Limits "Austin City Limits")*.[[17]](#cite_note-motto-18)[[18]](#cite_note-atx_motto-19) Austin is the site of [South by Southwest](/wiki/South_by_Southwest "South by Southwest") (SXSW), an annual conglomeration of parallel [film](/wiki/Film_festival "Film festival"), [interactive media](/wiki/Interactive_media "Interactive media"), and [music festivals](/wiki/Music_festival "Music festival"). The city also adopted "[Silicon Hills](/wiki/Silicon_Hills "Silicon Hills")" as a nickname in the 1990s due to a rapid influx of technology and development companies. In recent years, some Austinites have adopted the unofficial slogan "[Keep Austin Weird](/wiki/Keep_Austin_Weird "Keep Austin Weird")",[[19]](#cite_note-20) which refers to the desire to protect small, unique, and local businesses from being overrun by large corporations.[[20]](#cite_note-weird-21) Ongoing rapid development and [gentrification](/wiki/Gentrification "Gentrification") challenge its [bohemian](/wiki/Bohemianism "Bohemianism") roots and fuel nostalgia for “Old Austin.” Since the late 19th century, Austin has also been known as the "[City of the Violet Crown](/wiki/City_of_the_Violet_Crown "City of the Violet Crown")", because of the colorful glow of light across the hills just after sunset.[[21]](#cite_note-22)

Emerging from a strong economic focus on government and education, Austin has become a center for technology and business since the 1990s.[[22]](#cite_note-23)[[23]](#cite_note-austin_history-24) The technology roots in Austin can be traced back to the 1960s, when defense electronics contractor [Tracor](/wiki/Tracor "Tracor") (now [BAE Systems](/wiki/BAE_Systems "BAE Systems")) began operations in the city in 1962. IBM followed in 1967, opening a facility to produce its Selectric typewriters. Texas Instruments was set up in Austin two years later, and Motorola (now NXP Semiconductors) started semiconductor chip manufacturing in 1974. A number of [Fortune 500](/wiki/Fortune_500 "Fortune 500") companies have headquarters or regional offices in Austin, including [3M](/wiki/3M "3M"), [Advanced Micro Devices (AMD)](/wiki/Advanced_Micro_Devices "Advanced Micro Devices"), [Agilent Technologies](/wiki/Agilent_Technologies "Agilent Technologies"), [Amazon](/wiki/Amazon.com "Amazon.com"), [Apple](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc."), [CrowdStrike](/wiki/CrowdStrike "CrowdStrike"), [Dell](/wiki/Dell "Dell"), [Expedia](/wiki/Expedia_Group "Expedia Group"), [Facebook (Meta)](/wiki/Meta_Platforms "Meta Platforms"), [General Motors](/wiki/General_Motors "General Motors"), [Google](/wiki/Google "Google"), [IBM](/wiki/IBM "IBM"), [Intel](/wiki/Intel "Intel"), [NXP Semiconductors](/wiki/NXP_Semiconductors "NXP Semiconductors"), [Oracle](/wiki/Oracle_Corporation "Oracle Corporation"), [Tesla](/wiki/Tesla,_Inc. "Tesla, Inc."), and [Texas Instruments](/wiki/Texas_Instruments "Texas Instruments").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] With regard to education, Austin is the home of the [University of Texas at Austin](/wiki/University_of_Texas_at_Austin "University of Texas at Austin"), one of the largest universities in the U.S., with over 50,000 students.[[24]](#cite_note-25) In 2021, Austin became home to [Austin FC](/wiki/Austin_FC "Austin FC"), the first (and currently only) [major professional sports team](/wiki/Major_professional_sports_leagues_in_the_United_States_and_Canada "Major professional sports leagues in the United States and Canada") in the city.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Austin, Texas](/wiki/History_of_Austin,_Texas "History of Austin, Texas")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of Austin, Texas](/wiki/Timeline_of_Austin,_Texas "Timeline of Austin, Texas").

Austin, Travis County and Williamson County have been the site of human habitation since at least 9200 BC. The area's earliest known inhabitants lived during the late [Pleistocene](/wiki/Pleistocene "Pleistocene") (Ice Age) and are linked to the [Clovis culture](/wiki/Clovis_culture "Clovis culture") around 9200 BC (over 11,200 years ago), based on evidence found throughout the area and documented at the much-studied [Gault Site](/wiki/Gault_(archaeological_site) "Gault (archaeological site)"), midway between [Georgetown](/wiki/Georgetown,_Texas "Georgetown, Texas") and [Fort Hood](/wiki/Fort_Hood "Fort Hood").[[25]](#cite_note-26)[[26]](#cite_note-27)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

When settlers arrived from Europe, the [Tonkawa](/wiki/Tonkawa "Tonkawa") tribe inhabited the area. The [Comanches](/wiki/Comanche "Comanche") and [Lipan Apaches](/wiki/Lipan_Apache_people "Lipan Apache people") were also known to travel through the area.[[27]](#cite_note-28) Spanish colonists, including the [Espinosa](/wiki/Isidro_de_Espinosa "Isidro de Espinosa")-[Olivares](/wiki/Antonio_de_Olivares "Antonio de Olivares")-[Aguirre](/wiki/Pedro_de_Aguirre "Pedro de Aguirre") expedition, traveled through the area, though few permanent settlements were created for some time.[[28]](#cite_note-HOT:_Hays_Co-29) In 1730, three [Catholic missions](/wiki/Catholic_missions "Catholic missions") from [East Texas](/wiki/East_Texas "East Texas") were combined and reestablished as one mission on the south side of the Colorado River, in what is now [Zilker Park](/wiki/Zilker_Park "Zilker Park"), in Austin. The mission was in this area for only about seven months, then was moved to [San Antonio de Béxar](/wiki/San_Antonio_de_B%C3%A9xar "San Antonio de Béxar") and split into three missions.[[29]](#cite_note-30)

During the 1830s, pioneers began to settle the area in central Austin along the [Colorado River](/wiki/Colorado_River_(Texas) "Colorado River (Texas)"). Spanish forts were established in what are now [Bastrop](/wiki/Bastrop,_Texas "Bastrop, Texas") and [San Marcos](/wiki/San_Marcos,_Texas "San Marcos, Texas").[[28]](#cite_note-HOT:_Hays_Co-29)[[30]](#cite_note-HOT:_Bastrop-31) Following [Mexico's independence](/wiki/History_of_Mexico#Independence_era_(1808–1829) "History of Mexico"), new settlements were established in [Central Texas](/wiki/Central_Texas "Central Texas").[[30]](#cite_note-HOT:_Bastrop-31)[[31]](#cite_note-32)[[32]](#cite_note-HOT:Travis_Co-33)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Texas_capitol_goddess_1888.jpg/250px-Texas_capitol_goddess_1888.jpg)](/wiki/File:Texas_capitol_goddess_1888.jpg)

Statue of the Goddess of Liberty on the [Texas State Capitol](/wiki/Texas_State_Capitol "Texas State Capitol") grounds, prior to installation atop the rotunda

In 1835–1836, Texans fought and won [independence from Mexico](/wiki/Texas_Revolution "Texas Revolution"). Texas thus became an independent country with its own president, congress, and monetary system. In 1839, the Texas Congress formed a commission to seek a site for the new capital of the [Republic of Texas](/wiki/Republic_of_Texas "Republic of Texas") to replace [Houston](/wiki/Houston "Houston").[[33]](#cite_note-HOT:_Austin-34) When he was Vice President of Texas, [Mirabeau B. Lamar](/wiki/Mirabeau_B._Lamar "Mirabeau B. Lamar") had visited the area during a [buffalo](/wiki/American_bison "American bison")-hunting expedition between 1837 and 1838. He advised the commissioners to consider the area on the north bank of the [Colorado River](/wiki/Colorado_River_(Texas) "Colorado River (Texas)") (near the present-day [Congress Avenue Bridge](/wiki/Congress_Avenue_Bridge "Congress Avenue Bridge")), noting the area's hills, waterways, and pleasant surroundings.[[34]](#cite_note-Patoski_2010-35) It was seen as a convenient crossroads for trade routes between [Santa Fe](/wiki/Santa_Fe,_New_Mexico "Santa Fe, New Mexico") and [Galveston Bay](/wiki/Galveston_Bay "Galveston Bay"), as well as routes between Northern Mexico and the [Red River](/wiki/Red_River_of_the_South "Red River of the South").[[35]](#cite_note-Erlichman_2006-36) In 1839, the site was chosen, and briefly incorporated under the name "Waterloo".[[36]](#cite_note-37) Shortly afterward, the name was changed to Austin in honor of [Stephen F. Austin](/wiki/Stephen_F._Austin "Stephen F. Austin"), the "Father of Texas" and the republic's first secretary of state.

The city grew throughout the 19th century and became a center for government and education with the construction of the [Texas State Capitol](/wiki/Texas_State_Capitol "Texas State Capitol") and the [University of Texas at Austin](/wiki/University_of_Texas_at_Austin "University of Texas at Austin").[[37]](#cite_note-38)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Austin_old_capital.png/250px-Austin_old_capital.png)](/wiki/File:Austin_old_capital.png)

Second capitol building in Austin

[Edwin Waller](/wiki/Edwin_Waller "Edwin Waller") was picked by Lamar to survey the village and draft a plan laying out the new capital.[[33]](#cite_note-HOT:_Austin-34) The original site was narrowed to 640 acres (260 ha) that fronted the Colorado River between two creeks, [Shoal Creek](/wiki/Shoal_Creek,_Austin,_Texas "Shoal Creek, Austin, Texas") and [Waller Creek](/wiki/Waller_Creek "Waller Creek"), which was later named in his honor. Waller and a team of surveyors developed Austin's first [city plan](/wiki/City_plan "City plan"), commonly known as the [Waller Plan](/wiki/Waller_Plan "Waller Plan"), dividing the site into a 14-block grid plan bisected by a broad north–south thoroughfare, Congress Avenue, running up from the river to Capital Square, where the new Texas State Capitol was to be constructed. A temporary one-story capitol was erected on the corner of Colorado and 8th Streets. On August 1, 1839, the first auction of 217 out of 306 lots total was held.[[33]](#cite_note-HOT:_Austin-34)[[35]](#cite_note-Erlichman_2006-36) The Waller Plan designed and surveyed now forms the basis of downtown Austin.

In 1840, a series of conflicts between the [Texas Rangers](/wiki/Texas_Ranger_Division "Texas Ranger Division") and the [Comanches](/wiki/Comanche "Comanche"), known as the [Council House Fight](/wiki/Council_House_Fight "Council House Fight") and the [Battle of Plum Creek](/wiki/Battle_of_Plum_Creek "Battle of Plum Creek"), pushed the Comanches westward, mostly ending conflicts in Central Texas.[[38]](#cite_note-39) Settlement in the area began to expand quickly. Travis County was established in 1840, and the surrounding counties were mostly established within the next two decades.[[32]](#cite_note-HOT:Travis_Co-33)

Initially, the new capital thrived but Lamar's political enemy, [Sam Houston](/wiki/Sam_Houston "Sam Houston"), used two Mexican army incursions to [San Antonio](/wiki/San_Antonio "San Antonio") as an excuse to move the government. Sam Houston fought bitterly against Lamar's decision to establish the capital in such a remote wilderness. The men and women who traveled mainly from Houston to conduct government business were intensely disappointed as well. By 1840, the population had risen to 856, nearly half of whom fled Austin when Congress recessed.[[39]](#cite_note-40) The resident [African American](/wiki/African_American "African American") population listed in January of this same year was 176.[[40]](#cite_note-41) The fear of Austin's proximity to the Indians and Mexico, which still considered Texas a part of their land, created an immense motive for Sam Houston, the first and third President of the Republic of Texas, to relocate the capital once again in 1841. Upon threats of Mexican troops in Texas, Houston raided the Land Office to transfer all official documents to Houston for safe keeping in what was later known as the [Archive War](/wiki/Archive_War "Archive War"), but the people of Austin would not allow this unaccompanied decision to be executed. The documents stayed, but the capital would temporarily move from Austin to Houston to [Washington-on-the-Brazos](/wiki/Washington-on-the-Brazos,_Texas "Washington-on-the-Brazos, Texas"). Without the governmental body, Austin's population declined to a low of only a few hundred people throughout the early 1840s. The voting by the fourth President of the Republic, [Anson Jones](/wiki/Anson_Jones "Anson Jones"), and Congress, who reconvened in Austin in 1845, settled the issue to keep Austin the seat of government, as well as annex the Republic of Texas into the United States.

In 1860, 38% of Travis County residents were [slaves](/wiki/Slavery_in_the_United_States "Slavery in the United States").[[41]](#cite_note-42) In 1861, with the outbreak of the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), voters in Austin and other Central Texas communities voted against secession.[[30]](#cite_note-HOT:_Bastrop-31)[[33]](#cite_note-HOT:_Austin-34) However, as the war progressed and fears of attack by [Union](/wiki/Union_(American_Civil_War) "Union (American Civil War)") forces increased, Austin contributed hundreds of men to the [Confederate](/wiki/Confederate_States_of_America "Confederate States of America") forces. The African American population of Austin swelled dramatically after the enforcement of the [Emancipation Proclamation](/wiki/Emancipation_Proclamation "Emancipation Proclamation") in Texas by [Union](/wiki/Union_Army "Union Army") General [Gordon Granger](/wiki/Gordon_Granger "Gordon Granger") at [Galveston](/wiki/Galveston,_Texas "Galveston, Texas"), in an event commemorated as [Juneteenth](/wiki/Juneteenth "Juneteenth"). Black communities such as [Wheatville](/wiki/Wheatville,_Austin "Wheatville, Austin"), Pleasant Hill, and Clarksville were established, with Clarksville being the oldest surviving freedomtown ‒ the original post-Civil War settlements founded by former African-American slaves ‒ west of the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River").[[33]](#cite_note-HOT:_Austin-34) In 1870, blacks made up 36.5% of Austin's population.[[42]](#cite_note-census-43)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Old_map-Austin-1873-sm.jpg/250px-Old_map-Austin-1873-sm.jpg)](/wiki/File:Old_map-Austin-1873-sm.jpg)

An 1873 illustration of [Edwin Waller](/wiki/Edwin_Waller "Edwin Waller")'s layout for Austin

The postwar period saw dramatic population and economic growth. The opening of the [Houston and Texas Central Railway](/wiki/Houston_and_Texas_Central_Railway "Houston and Texas Central Railway") (H&TC) in 1871[[43]](#cite_note-44) turned Austin into the major trading center for the region, with the ability to transport both cotton and cattle. The [Missouri, Kansas & Texas](/wiki/Missouri%E2%80%93Kansas%E2%80%93Texas_Railroad "Missouri–Kansas–Texas Railroad") (MKT) line followed close behind.[[44]](#cite_note-45) Austin was also the terminus of the southernmost leg of the [Chisholm Trail](/wiki/Chisholm_Trail "Chisholm Trail"), and "drovers" pushed cattle north to the railroad.[[45]](#cite_note-46) Cotton was one of the few crops produced locally for export, and a [cotton gin](/wiki/Cotton_gin "Cotton gin") engine was located downtown near the trains for "ginning" cotton of its seeds and turning the product into bales for shipment.[[46]](#cite_note-47) However, as other new railroads were built through the region in the 1870s, Austin began to lose its primacy in trade to the surrounding communities.[[33]](#cite_note-HOT:_Austin-34) In addition, the areas east of Austin took over cattle and cotton production from Austin, especially in towns like [Hutto](/wiki/Hutto,_Texas "Hutto, Texas") and [Taylor](/wiki/Taylor,_Texas "Taylor, Texas") that sit over the [blackland prairie](/wiki/Texas_blackland_prairies "Texas blackland prairies"), with its deep, rich soils for producing cotton and hay.[[47]](#cite_note-48)[[48]](#cite_note-49)

In September 1881, Austin public schools held their first classes. The same year, Tillotson Collegiate and Normal Institute (now part of [Huston–Tillotson University](/wiki/Huston%E2%80%93Tillotson_University "Huston–Tillotson University")) opened its doors. The [University of Texas](/wiki/University_of_Texas_at_Austin "University of Texas at Austin") held its first classes in 1883, although classes had been held in the original wooden state capitol for four years before.[[49]](#cite_note-50)

During the 1880s, Austin gained new prominence as the [state capitol building](/wiki/Texas_State_Capitol "Texas State Capitol") was completed in 1888 and claimed as the seventh largest building in the world.[[33]](#cite_note-HOT:_Austin-34) In the late 19th century, Austin expanded its city limits to more than three times its former area, and the first granite dam was built on the Colorado River to power a new street car line and the new "[moon towers](/wiki/Moonlight_tower "Moonlight tower")".[[33]](#cite_note-HOT:_Austin-34) The first dam washed away in a flood on April 7, 1900.[[50]](#cite_note-51)

In the late 1920s and 1930s, Austin implemented the [1928 Austin city plan](/wiki/1928_Austin_city_plan "1928 Austin city plan") through a series of civic development and beautification projects that created much of the city's infrastructure and many of its parks. In addition, the state legislature established the [Lower Colorado River Authority](/wiki/Lower_Colorado_River_Authority "Lower Colorado River Authority") (LCRA) that, along with the city of Austin, created the system of dams along the Colorado River to form the [Highland Lakes](/wiki/Texas_Highland_Lakes "Texas Highland Lakes"). These projects were enabled in large part because the [Public Works Administration](/wiki/Public_Works_Administration "Public Works Administration") provided Austin with greater funding for municipal construction projects than other Texas cities.[[33]](#cite_note-HOT:_Austin-34)

During the early 20th century, a three-way system of social segregation emerged in Austin, with Anglos, African Americans and Mexicans being separated by custom or law in most aspects of life, including housing, health care, and education. Deed restrictions also played an important role in [residential segregation](/wiki/Residential_segregation_in_the_United_States "Residential segregation in the United States"). After 1935 most housing deeds prohibited African Americans (and sometimes other nonwhite groups) from using land.[[51]](#cite_note-52) Combined with the system of segregated public services, racial segregation increased in Austin during the first half of the twentieth century, with African Americans and Mexicans experiencing high levels of discrimination and social marginalization.[[52]](#cite_note-53)

In 1940, the destroyed granite dam on the Colorado River was finally replaced by a hollow concrete dam[[53]](#cite_note-54) that formed Lake McDonald (now called [Lake Austin](/wiki/Lake_Austin "Lake Austin")) and which has withstood all floods since. In addition, the much larger Mansfield Dam was built by the LCRA upstream of Austin to form Lake Travis, a flood-control reservoir.[[54]](#cite_note-55)

In the early 20th century, the [Texas Oil Boom](/wiki/Texas_Oil_Boom "Texas Oil Boom") took hold, creating tremendous economic opportunities in Southeast Texas and North Texas. The growth generated by this boom largely passed by Austin at first, with the city slipping from fourth largest to tenth largest in Texas between 1880 and 1920.[[33]](#cite_note-HOT:_Austin-34)

After a severe lull in economic growth from the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression_in_the_United_States "Great Depression in the United States"), Austin resumed its steady development. Following the mid-20th century, Austin became established as one of Texas' major metropolitan centers. In 1970, the U.S. Census Bureau reported Austin's population as 14.5% Hispanic, 11.9% black, and 73.4% non-Hispanic white.[[42]](#cite_note-census-43) In the late 20th century, Austin emerged as an important high tech center for [semiconductors](/wiki/Semiconductor "Semiconductor") and software. The [University of Texas at Austin](/wiki/University_of_Texas_at_Austin "University of Texas at Austin") emerged as a major university.[[55]](#cite_note-56)

The 1970s saw Austin's emergence in the national music scene, with local artists such as [Willie Nelson](/wiki/Willie_Nelson "Willie Nelson"), [Asleep at the Wheel](/wiki/Asleep_at_the_Wheel "Asleep at the Wheel"), and [Stevie Ray Vaughan](/wiki/Stevie_Ray_Vaughan "Stevie Ray Vaughan") and iconic music venues such as the [Armadillo World Headquarters](/wiki/Armadillo_World_Headquarters "Armadillo World Headquarters"). Over time, the long-running television program *Austin City Limits*, its namesake Austin City Limits Festival, and the [South by Southwest](/wiki/South_by_Southwest "South by Southwest") music festival solidified the city's place in the music industry.[[23]](#cite_note-austin_history-24)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Austin_by_Sentinel-2%2C_2020-03-25.jpg/250px-Austin_by_Sentinel-2%2C_2020-03-25.jpg)](/wiki/File:Austin_by_Sentinel-2,_2020-03-25.jpg)

Austin as seen from space, 2020

Austin, the southernmost state capital of the contiguous 48 states, is located in [Central Texas](/wiki/Central_Texas "Central Texas") on the [Colorado River](/wiki/Colorado_River_(Texas) "Colorado River (Texas)"). Austin is 146 miles (230 km) northwest of [Houston](/wiki/Houston "Houston"),[[56]](#cite_note-57) 182 miles (290 km) south of [Dallas](/wiki/Dallas "Dallas"),[[57]](#cite_note-58) and 74 miles (120 km) northeast of [San Antonio](/wiki/San_Antonio "San Antonio").[[58]](#cite_note-59)

Austin occupies a total area of 305.1 square miles (790.1 km2). Approximately 7.2 square miles (18.6 km2) of this area is water.[[7]](#cite_note-Census_2010-8) Austin is situated at the foot of the [Balcones Escarpment](/wiki/Balcones_Escarpment "Balcones Escarpment"), on the [Colorado River](/wiki/Colorado_River_(Texas) "Colorado River (Texas)"), with three artificial lakes within the city limits: [Lady Bird Lake](/wiki/Lady_Bird_Lake "Lady Bird Lake") (formerly known as Town Lake), Lake Austin (both created by dams along the Colorado River), and Lake Walter E. Long that is partly used for cooling water for the Decker Power Plant. [Mansfield Dam](/wiki/Mansfield_Dam "Mansfield Dam") and the foot of [Lake Travis](/wiki/Lake_Travis "Lake Travis") are located within the city's limits.[[33]](#cite_note-HOT:_Austin-34) Lady Bird Lake, Lake Austin, and Lake Travis are each on the Colorado River.[[33]](#cite_note-HOT:_Austin-34)

The elevation of Austin varies from 425 feet (130 m) to approximately 1,000 feet (305 m) above sea level.[[59]](#cite_note-Abbott_2003-60) Due to the fact it straddles the [Balcones Fault](/wiki/Balcones_Fault "Balcones Fault"), much of the eastern part of the city is flat, with heavy clay and loam soils, whereas the western part and western suburbs consist of rolling hills on the edge of the [Texas Hill Country](/wiki/Texas_Hill_Country "Texas Hill Country").[[60]](#cite_note-Baird_2009-61) Because the hills to the west are primarily [limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone") rock with a thin covering of topsoil, portions of the city are frequently subjected to [flash floods](/wiki/Flash_flood "Flash flood") from the runoff caused by thunderstorms.[[61]](#cite_note-62)[[62]](#cite_note-63) To help control this runoff and to generate hydroelectric power, the [Lower Colorado River Authority](/wiki/Lower_Colorado_River_Authority "Lower Colorado River Authority") operates a series of dams that form the [Texas Highland Lakes](/wiki/Texas_Highland_Lakes "Texas Highland Lakes"). The lakes also provide venues for boating, swimming, and other forms of recreation within several parks on the lake shores.[[63]](#cite_note-64)

Austin is located at the intersection of four major ecological regions, and is consequently a temperate-to-hot green oasis with a highly variable climate having some characteristics of the desert, the tropics, and a wetter climate.[[64]](#cite_note-65)[[65]](#cite_note-66) The area is very diverse ecologically and biologically, and is home to a variety of animals and plants.[[66]](#cite_note-Vines_1984-67) Notably, the area is home to many types of wildflowers that blossom throughout the year but especially in the spring. This includes the popular [bluebonnets](/wiki/Bluebonnet_(plant) "Bluebonnet (plant)"), some planted by ["Lady Bird" Johnson](/wiki/Lady_Bird_Johnson "Lady Bird Johnson"), wife of former President [Lyndon B. Johnson](/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson "Lyndon B. Johnson").[[67]](#cite_note-68)

The soils of Austin range from shallow, gravelly clay loams over limestone in the western outskirts to deep, fine sandy loams, silty clay loams, silty clays or clays in the city's eastern part. Some of the clays have pronounced shrink-swell properties and are difficult to work under most moisture conditions. Many of Austin's soils, especially the clay-rich types, are slightly to moderately alkaline and have free [calcium carbonate](/wiki/Calcium_carbonate "Calcium carbonate").[[68]](#cite_note-69)

### Cityscape

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Cityscape")]

See also: [List of Austin neighborhoods](/wiki/List_of_Austin_neighborhoods "List of Austin neighborhoods") and [List of tallest buildings in Austin, Texas](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Austin,_Texas "List of tallest buildings in Austin, Texas")

[![Downtown Austin, Texas from across the Colorado River, October 2022](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Downtown_Austin%2C_Texas_from_the_Colorado_River%2C_October_2022.jpg/960px-Downtown_Austin%2C_Texas_from_the_Colorado_River%2C_October_2022.jpg)](/wiki/File:Downtown_Austin,_Texas_from_the_Colorado_River,_October_2022.jpg "Downtown Austin, Texas from across the Colorado River, October 2022")

Downtown Austin, Texas from across the Colorado River, October 2022

Austin's skyline historically was modest, dominated by the Texas State Capitol and the University of Texas [Main Building](/wiki/Main_Building_(University_of_Texas_at_Austin) "Main Building (University of Texas at Austin)"). However, since the 2000s, many new high-rise towers have been constructed.[[69]](#cite_note-70) Austin is currently undergoing a skyscraper boom, which includes recent construction on new office, hotel and residential buildings. Downtown's buildings are somewhat spread out, partly due to a set of [zoning](/wiki/Zoning "Zoning") restrictions that preserve the view of the [Texas State Capitol](/wiki/Texas_State_Capitol "Texas State Capitol") from various locations around Austin, known as the [Capitol View Corridors](/wiki/Texas_Capitol_View_Corridors "Texas Capitol View Corridors").[[70]](#cite_note-71)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/9162018.Moon.Light.towers.NRSID76002071_02.jpg/250px-9162018.Moon.Light.towers.NRSID76002071_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:9162018.Moon.Light.towers.NRSID76002071_02.jpg)

One of the 15 remaining [moonlight towers](/wiki/Moonlight_towers_(Austin,_Texas) "Moonlight towers (Austin, Texas)") in Austin

At night, parts of Austin are lit by "artificial moonlight" from [moonlight towers](/wiki/Moonlight_tower "Moonlight tower") built to illuminate the central part of the city.[[71]](#cite_note-72) The 165-foot (50 m) moonlight towers were built in the late 19th century and are now recognized as historic landmarks. Only 15 of the 31 original innovative towers remain standing in Austin, but none remain in any of the other cities where they were installed. The towers are featured in the 1993 film *[Dazed and Confused](/wiki/Dazed_and_Confused_(film) "Dazed and Confused (film)")*.

In December 2023, amid rising home prices, the Austin City Council loosened the city's zoning rules to permit by-right development of triplexes on each lot and loosened restrictions on tiny homes.[[72]](#cite_note-73)

#### Downtown

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Downtown")]

Main article: [Downtown Austin](/wiki/Downtown_Austin "Downtown Austin")

The central business district of Austin is home to the tallest condo towers in the state, with [The Independent](/wiki/The_Independent_(Austin,_Texas) "The Independent (Austin, Texas)") (58 stories and 690 ft (210 m) tall) and [The Austonian](/wiki/The_Austonian "The Austonian") (topping out at 56 floors and 685 ft (209 m) tall). The Independent became the tallest all-residential building in the U.S. west of [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") when topped out in 2018. In 2005, then-Mayor Will Wynn set out a goal of having 25,000 people living downtown by 2015.[[73]](#cite_note-74) Although downtown's growth did not meet this goal, downtown's residential population did surge from an estimated 5,000 in 2005 to 12,000 in 2015.[[74]](#cite_note-75) The skyline has drastically changed in recent years, and the residential real estate market has remained relatively strong. As of December 2016[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit), there were 31 high rise projects either under construction, approved or planned to be completed in Austin's downtown core between 2017 and 2020. Sixteen of those were set to rise above 400 ft (120 m) tall, including four above 600', and eight above 500'. An additional 15 towers were slated to stand between 300' and 399' tall.

### Climate

Austin is located within the middle of a unique, narrow transitional zone between the dry deserts of the American Southwest and the lush, green, more humid regions of the American Southeast. Its climate, topography, and vegetation share characteristics of both. Officially, Austin has a [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") (*Cfa* under the [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"), *Cfhl* under the [Trewartha climate classification](/wiki/Trewartha_climate_classification "Trewartha climate classification")). This climate is typified by long, very hot summers, short, mild winters, and warm to hot spring and fall seasons in-between. Austin averages 34.32 inches (872 mm) of annual rainfall distributed mostly evenly throughout the year, though spring and fall are the wettest seasons. Sunshine is common during all seasons, with 2,650 hours, or 60.3% of the possible total, of bright sunshine per year.[[75]](#cite_note-76)

Summers in Austin are very hot, with average July and August highs frequently reaching the high-90s (34–36 °C) or above. Highs reach 90 °F (32 °C) on 123 days per year, of which 29 days reach 100 °F (38 °C); all years in the 1991-2020 period recorded at least 1 day of the latter.[[76]](#cite_note-NWSC-NOAA-77) The average daytime high is 70 °F (21 °C) or warmer between March 1 and November 21, rising to 80 °F (27 °C) or warmer between April 14 and October 24, and reaching 90 °F (32 °C) or warmer between May 30 and September 18.[[76]](#cite_note-NWSC-NOAA-77) The highest ever recorded temperature was 112 °F (44 °C) occurring on September 5, 2000, and August 28, 2011.[[77]](#cite_note-78)[[78]](#cite_note-79) An uncommon characteristic of Austin's climate is its highly variable humidity, which fluctuates frequently depending on the shifting patterns of air flow and wind direction. It is common for a lengthy series of warm, dry, low-humidity days to be occasionally interrupted by very warm and humid days, and vice versa. Humidity rises with winds from the east or southeast, when the air drifts inland from the [Gulf of Mexico](/wiki/Gulf_of_Mexico "Gulf of Mexico"), but decreases significantly with winds from the west or southwest, bringing air flowing from [Chihuahuan Desert](/wiki/Chihuahuan_Desert "Chihuahuan Desert") areas of [West Texas](/wiki/West_Texas "West Texas") or Northern Mexico.[[76]](#cite_note-NWSC-NOAA-77)

Winters in Austin are mild, although occasional short-lived bursts of cold weather known as "[Blue Northers](/wiki/Texas_Norther "Texas Norther")" can occur. January is the coolest month with an average daytime high of 62.5 °F (17 °C). The overnight low drops to or below freezing 12 times per year,[[76]](#cite_note-NWSC-NOAA-77) and sinks below 45 °F (7 °C) during 76 evenings per year, mostly between mid-December and mid-February. The average first and last dates for a freeze are December 1 and February 15, giving Austin an average growing season of 288 days, and the coldest temperature of the year is normally about 24.2 °F (−4 °C) under the 1991-2020 climate normals, putting Austin in USDA zone 9a.[[76]](#cite_note-NWSC-NOAA-77)

Conversely, winter months also produce warm days on a regular basis. On average, 10 days in January reach or exceed 70 °F (21 °C) and 1 day reaches 80 °F (27 °C); during the 1991-2020 period, all Januarys had at least 1 day with a high of 70 °F (21 °C) or more, and most (60%) had at least 1 day with a high of 80 °F (27 °C) or more.[[76]](#cite_note-NWSC-NOAA-77) The lowest ever recorded temperature in the city was −2 °F (−19 °C) on January 31, 1949. Roughly every two years Austin experiences an [ice storm](/wiki/Ice_storm "Ice storm") that freezes roads over and cripples travel in the city for 24 to 48 hours.[[79]](#cite_note-80) When Austin received 0.04 inches (1 mm) of ice on January 24, 2014, there were 278 vehicular collisions.[[80]](#cite_note-81) Similarly, snowfall is rare in Austin.[[81]](#cite_note-82) A snow event of 0.9 inches (2 cm) on February 4, 2011, caused more than 300 car crashes.[[82]](#cite_note-83) The most recent major snow event occurred February 14–15, 2021, when 6.4 inches (16 cm) of snow fell at Austin's [Camp Mabry](/wiki/Camp_Mabry "Camp Mabry"), the largest two-day snowfall since records began being kept in 1948.[[83]](#cite_note-84)[[84]](#cite_note-85)

Typical of [Central Texas](/wiki/Central_Texas "Central Texas"), severe weather in Austin is a threat that can strike during any season. However, it is most common during the spring. According to most classifications, Austin lies within the extreme southern periphery of [Tornado Alley](/wiki/Tornado_Alley "Tornado Alley"), although many sources place Austin outside of Tornado Alley altogether.[[85]](#cite_note-Data_Foundry:_Austin_Risk_Assessment-86) Consequently, tornadoes strike Austin less frequently than areas farther to the north.[[85]](#cite_note-Data_Foundry:_Austin_Risk_Assessment-86) However, severe weather and/or [supercell thunderstorms](/wiki/Supercell "Supercell") can occur multiple times per year, bringing damaging winds, lightning, heavy rain, and occasional flash flooding to the city.[[86]](#cite_note-87) The deadliest storm to ever strike city limits was the [twin tornadoes storm](/wiki/1922_Austin_twin_tornadoes "1922 Austin twin tornadoes") of May 4, 1922, while the deadliest [tornado outbreak](/wiki/Tornado_outbreak "Tornado outbreak") to ever strike the metro area was the [Central Texas tornado outbreak](/wiki/1997_Central_Texas_tornado_outbreak "1997 Central Texas tornado outbreak") of May 27, 1997.

### Natural disasters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Natural disasters")]

#### 2011 drought

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2011 drought")]

Main article: [2011 Southern US drought](/wiki/2011_Southern_US_drought "2011 Southern US drought")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/2011_Texas_Drought.jpg/250px-2011_Texas_Drought.jpg)](/wiki/File:2011_Texas_Drought.jpg)

The 2011 Texas drought dried up many of central Texas' waterways. This boat was left to sit in the middle of what is normally a branch of [Lake Travis](/wiki/Lake_Travis "Lake Travis"), part of the [Colorado River](/wiki/Colorado_River_(Texas) "Colorado River (Texas)").

From October 2010 through September 2011, both major reporting stations in Austin, Camp Mabry and Bergstrom Int'l, had the least rainfall of a [water year](/wiki/Water_year "Water year") on record, receiving less than a third of normal precipitation.[[76]](#cite_note-NWSC-NOAA-77) This was a result of [La Niña](/wiki/La_Ni%C3%B1a "La Niña") conditions in the eastern Pacific Ocean where water was significantly cooler than normal. David Brown, a regional official with the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration, explained that "these kinds of droughts will have effects that are even more extreme in the future, given a warming and drying regional climate."[[91]](#cite_note-94) The drought, coupled with exceedingly high temperatures throughout the summer of 2011, caused many wildfires throughout Texas, including notably the [Bastrop County Complex Fire](/wiki/Bastrop_County_Complex_Fire "Bastrop County Complex Fire") in neighboring Bastrop, Texas.

#### 2018 flooding and water crisis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2018 flooding and water crisis")]

Main article: [Llano River § 2018 Flood](/wiki/Llano_River#2018_Flood "Llano River")

In the fall of 2018, Austin and surrounding areas received heavy rainfall and [flash flooding](/wiki/Flash_flood "Flash flood") following [Hurricane Sergio](/wiki/Hurricane_Sergio_(2018) "Hurricane Sergio (2018)").[[92]](#cite_note-rainfall-95) The [Lower Colorado River Authority](/wiki/Lower_Colorado_River_Authority "Lower Colorado River Authority") opened four [floodgates](/wiki/Floodgates "Floodgates") of the [Mansfield Dam](/wiki/Mansfield_Dam "Mansfield Dam") after Lake Travis was recorded at 146% full at 704.3 feet (214.7 m).[[93]](#cite_note-96) From October 22 to October 29, 2018, the City of Austin issued a mandatory citywide [boil-water advisory](/wiki/Boil-water_advisory "Boil-water advisory") after the [Highland Lakes](/wiki/Texas_Highland_Lakes "Texas Highland Lakes"), home to the city's main water supply, became overwhelmed by unprecedented amounts of silt, dirt, and debris that had washed in from the [Llano River](/wiki/Llano_River "Llano River").[[94]](#cite_note-97) Austin Water, the city's water utility, has the capacity to process up to 300 million gallons of water per day; however, the elevated level of [turbidity](/wiki/Turbidity "Turbidity") reduced output to only 105 million gallons per day. Since Austin residents consumed an average of 120 million gallons of water per day, the infrastructure was not able to keep up with demand.[[92]](#cite_note-rainfall-95)

#### 2021 winter storm

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2021 winter storm")]

Main article: [February 13–17, 2021 North American winter storm § Central and Southern Plains](/wiki/February_13%E2%80%9317,_2021_North_American_winter_storm#Central_and_Southern_Plains "February 13–17, 2021 North American winter storm")

See also: [2021 Texas power crisis](/wiki/2021_Texas_power_crisis "2021 Texas power crisis")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Austin%2C_Texas_covered_in_snow_ESA23166430.jpeg/250px-Austin%2C_Texas_covered_in_snow_ESA23166430.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Austin,_Texas_covered_in_snow_ESA23166430.jpeg)

Austin covered in snow on February 15, 2021. Photo from [ESA](/wiki/ESA "ESA").

In February 2021, [Winter Storm Uri](/wiki/Winter_Storm_Uri "Winter Storm Uri") dropped prolific amounts of snow across Texas and Oklahoma, including Austin. The Austin area received a total of 6.4 inches (160 mm) of snowfall between February 14 and 15, with snow cover persisting until February 20.[[95]](#cite_note-98)
This marked the longest time the area had had more than 1 inch (25 mm) of snow, with the previous longest time being three days in [January 1985](/wiki/1985_North_American_cold_wave "1985 North American cold wave").[[96]](#cite_note-99)

Lack of [winterization](/wiki/Winterization "Winterization") in [natural gas power plants](/wiki/Gas-fired_power_plant "Gas-fired power plant"), which supply a large amount of power to the [Texas grid](/wiki/Texas_Interconnection "Texas Interconnection"), and increased energy demand caused ERCOT and [Austin Energy](/wiki/Austin_Energy "Austin Energy") to enact [rolling blackouts](/wiki/Rolling_blackouts "Rolling blackouts") in order to avoid total grid collapse between February 15 and February 18.[[97]](#cite_note-100) Initial rolling blackouts were to last for a maximum of 40 minutes, however lack of energy production caused many blackouts to last for much longer, at the peak of the blackouts an estimated 40% of Austin Energy homes were without power.[[98]](#cite_note-101)

Starting on February 15, Austin Water received reports of pipe breaks, hourly water demand increased from 150 million gallons per day on February 15 to a peak hourly demand of 260 million gallons per day on February 16. On the morning of February 17 demand increased to 330 million gallons per day, the resulting drop of water pressure caused the Austin area to enter into a [boil-water advisory](/wiki/Boil-water_advisory "Boil-water advisory") which would last until water pressure was restored on February 23.[[99]](#cite_note-102)

#### 2023 winter storm

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 2023 winter storm")]

Main article: [January – February, 2023 ice storm](/wiki/January_%E2%80%93_February,_2023_ice_storm "January – February, 2023 ice storm")

Beginning January 30, 2023 the City of Austin experienced a winter freeze which left 170,000 Austin Energy customers without electricity or heat for several days. The slow pace of repairs and lack of public information from City officials frustrated many residents.[[100]](#cite_note-103) A week after the freeze and when Austin City Council members were proposing to evaluate his employment, City Manager Spencer Cronk finally apologized.[[101]](#cite_note-104) On Thursday February 16, 2023, Cronk was fired by the Austin City Council for the city's response to the winter storm.[[102]](#cite_note-105) Former City Manager Jesus Garcia was named Interim City Manager.[[103]](#cite_note-106)

### Parks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Parks")]

The Austin Parks and Recreation Department received the Excellence in Aquatics award in 1999 and the Gold Medal Awards in 2004 from the [National Recreation and Park Association](/wiki/National_Recreation_and_Park_Association "National Recreation and Park Association").[[104]](#cite_note-107)[[105]](#cite_note-108)

To strengthen the region's parks system, which spans more than 29,000 acres (11,736 ha), The Austin Parks Foundation was established in 1992 to develop and improve parks in and around Austin. APF works to fill the city's park funding gap by leveraging volunteers, philanthropists, park advocates, and strategic collaborations to develop, maintain and enhance Austin's parks, trails and green spaces.

### Lady Bird Lake

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Lady Bird Lake")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Hamilton_Pool_March_2016_3.JPG/250px-Hamilton_Pool_March_2016_3.JPG)](/wiki/File:Hamilton_Pool_March_2016_3.JPG)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Covert_Park_at_Mount_Bonnell_20160905130602.jpg/250px-Covert_Park_at_Mount_Bonnell_20160905130602.jpg)](/wiki/File:Covert_Park_at_Mount_Bonnell_20160905130602.jpg)

View of the Colorado River from Covert Park at [Mount Bonnell](/wiki/Mount_Bonnell "Mount Bonnell")

[Lady Bird Lake](/wiki/Lady_Bird_Lake "Lady Bird Lake") (formerly Town Lake)[[106]](#cite_note-109) is a river-like reservoir on the Colorado River. The lake is a popular recreational area for [paddleboards](/wiki/Paddleboarding "Paddleboarding"), [kayaks](/wiki/Kayak "Kayak"), [canoes](/wiki/Canoe "Canoe"), [dragon boats](/wiki/Dragon_boat "Dragon boat"), and [rowing shells](/wiki/Rowing_(sport) "Rowing (sport)"). Austin's warm climate and the river's calm waters, nearly 6 miles (9.7 km) length and straight courses are especially popular with [crew](/wiki/Sport_rowing "Sport rowing") teams and clubs. Other recreational attractions along the shores of the lake include swimming in [Deep Eddy Pool](/wiki/Deep_Eddy_Pool "Deep Eddy Pool"), the oldest swimming pool in Texas, and Red Bud Isle, a small island formed by the 1900 [collapse of the McDonald Dam](/wiki/Austin_Dam_failure_(Texas) "Austin Dam failure (Texas)") that serves as a recreation area with a dog park and access to the lake for canoeing and fishing.[[107]](#cite_note-110) The 10.1 miles (16.3 km) Ann and Roy Butler Hike and Bike Trail forms a complete circuit around the lake. A local nonprofit, The Trail Foundation, is the Trail's private steward and has built amenities and infrastructure including trailheads, lakefront gathering areas, restrooms, exercise equipment, as well as doing Trailwide [ecological restoration](/wiki/Ecological_restoration "Ecological restoration") work on an ongoing basis. The Butler Trail loop was completed in 2014 with the public-private partnership 1-mile Boardwalk project.[[108]](#cite_note-111)

Along the shores of Lady Bird Lake is the 350 acres (140 hectares) [Zilker Park](/wiki/Zilker_Park "Zilker Park"), which contains large open lawns, sports fields, cross country courses, historical markers, concession stands, and picnic areas.[[109]](#cite_note-112) Zilker Park is also home to numerous attractions, including the [Zilker Botanical Garden](/wiki/Zilker_Botanical_Garden "Zilker Botanical Garden"), the [Umlauf Sculpture Garden](/wiki/Umlauf_Sculpture_Garden "Umlauf Sculpture Garden"), Zilker Hillside Theater, the Austin Nature & Science Center, and the Zilker Zephyr, a 12 in (305 mm) gauge [miniature railway](/wiki/Ridable_miniature_railway "Ridable miniature railway") carries passengers on a tour around the park.[[110]](#cite_note-113)[[111]](#cite_note-114) [Auditorium Shores](/wiki/Auditorium_Shores "Auditorium Shores"), an [urban park](/wiki/Urban_park "Urban park") along the lake, is home to the [Palmer Auditorium](/wiki/Palmer_Auditorium "Palmer Auditorium"), the [Long Center for the Performing Arts](/wiki/Long_Center_for_the_Performing_Arts "Long Center for the Performing Arts"), and an [off-leash dog park](/wiki/Dog_park "Dog park") on the water.[[112]](#cite_note-115) Both Zilker Park and Auditorium Shores have a direct view of the Downtown skyline.[[113]](#cite_note-116)

### Barton Creek Greenbelt

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Barton Creek Greenbelt")]

The [Barton Creek Greenbelt](/wiki/Barton_Creek_Greenbelt "Barton Creek Greenbelt") is a 7.25-mile (11.67 km) public [green belt](/wiki/Green_belt "Green belt") managed by the City of Austin's Park and Recreation Department. The Greenbelt, which begins at [Zilker Park](/wiki/Zilker_Park "Zilker Park") and stretches South/Southwest to the Woods of Westlake [subdivision](/wiki/Subdivision_(land) "Subdivision (land)"), is characterized by large [limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone") cliffs, dense foliage, and shallow bodies of water. Popular activities include [rock climbing](/wiki/Rock_climbing "Rock climbing"), mountain biking, and hiking. Some well known naturally forming swimming holes along Austin's greenbelt include Twin Falls, Sculpture Falls, Gus Fruh Pool, and Campbell's Hole. During years of heavy rainfall, the water level of the creek rises high enough to allow [swimming](/wiki/Human_swimming "Human swimming"), [cliff diving](/wiki/High_diving "High diving"), [kayaking](/wiki/Kayaking "Kayaking"), [paddle boarding](/wiki/Paddleboarding "Paddleboarding"), and [tubing](/wiki/Tubing_(recreation) "Tubing (recreation)").

### Swimming holes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Swimming holes")]

Austin is home to more than 50 public pools and [swimming holes](/wiki/Swimming_hole "Swimming hole"). These include [Deep Eddy Pool](/wiki/Deep_Eddy_Pool "Deep Eddy Pool"), Texas' oldest human-made swimming pool, and [Barton Springs Pool](/wiki/Barton_Springs_Pool "Barton Springs Pool"), the nation's largest natural swimming pool in an urban area.[[114]](#cite_note-117)[[115]](#cite_note-118) Barton Springs Pool is spring-fed while Deep Eddy is well-fed. Both range in temperature from about 68.0 °F (20.0 °C) during the winter to about 71.6 °F (22.0 °C) during the summer.[[116]](#cite_note-119)[[117]](#cite_note-120)[[118]](#cite_note-121) [Hippie Hollow Park](/wiki/Hippie_Hollow_Park "Hippie Hollow Park"), a county park situated along Lake Travis, is the only officially sanctioned [clothing-optional public park](/wiki/Nude_beach "Nude beach") in Texas. [Hamilton Pool Preserve](/wiki/Hamilton_Pool_Preserve "Hamilton Pool Preserve") is a natural pool that was created when the dome of an underground river collapsed due to massive erosion thousands of years ago. The pool, located about 23 miles (37 km) west of Austin, is a popular summer swimming spot for visitors and residents. Hamilton Pool Preserve consists of 232 acres (0.94 km2) of protected natural habitat featuring a jade green pool into which a 50-foot (15 m) waterfall flows.[[119]](#cite_note-122)

#### Other parks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Other parks")]

In May 2021, voters in the City of Austin reinstated a public camping ban.[[120]](#cite_note-123) That includes downtown green spaces as well as trails and greenbelts such as along Barton Creek.[[d]](#cite_note-125)

[McKinney Falls State Park](/wiki/McKinney_Falls_State_Park "McKinney Falls State Park") is a [state park](/wiki/State_park "State park") administered by the [Texas Parks and Wildlife Department](/wiki/Texas_Parks_and_Wildlife_Department "Texas Parks and Wildlife Department"), located at the confluence of [Onion Creek](/wiki/Onion_Creek_(Texas) "Onion Creek (Texas)") and Williamson Creek. The park includes several designated hiking trails and [campsites](/wiki/Campground "Campground") with water and electric. The namesake features of the park are the scenic upper and lower falls along Onion Creek. The [Emma Long Metropolitan Park](/wiki/Emma_Long_Metropolitan_Park "Emma Long Metropolitan Park") is a municipal park along the shores of [Lake Austin](/wiki/Lake_Austin "Lake Austin"), originally constructed by the [Civilian Conservation Corps](/wiki/Civilian_Conservation_Corps "Civilian Conservation Corps"). The 284 acres (115 ha) [Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center](/wiki/Lady_Bird_Johnson_Wildflower_Center "Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center") is a [botanical garden](/wiki/Botanical_garden "Botanical garden") and [arboretum](/wiki/Arboretum "Arboretum") that features more than 800 species of native Texas plants in both garden and natural settings; the Wildflower Center is located 10 miles (16 km) southwest of Downtown in [Circle C Ranch](/wiki/Circle_C_Ranch "Circle C Ranch").[[122]](#cite_note-126) Roy G. Guerrero Park is located along the Colorado River in [East Riverside](/wiki/East_Riverside-Oltorf,_Austin,_Texas "East Riverside-Oltorf, Austin, Texas") and contains miles of wooded trails, a sandy beach along the river, and a [disc golf](/wiki/Disc_golf "Disc golf") course.

Covert Park, located on the top of [Mount Bonnell](/wiki/Mount_Bonnell "Mount Bonnell"), is a popular tourist destination overlooking Lake Austin and the Colorado River. The mount provides a vista for viewing the city of Austin, Lake Austin, and the surrounding hills.[[123]](#cite_note-127) It was designated a [Recorded Texas Historic Landmark](/wiki/Recorded_Texas_Historic_Landmark "Recorded Texas Historic Landmark") in 1969, bearing Marker number 6473,[[124]](#cite_note-128) and was listed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places") in 2015.

The [Louis René Barrera Indiangrass Wildlife Sanctuary](/wiki/Louis_Ren%C3%A9_Barrera_Indiangrass_Wildlife_Sanctuary "Louis René Barrera Indiangrass Wildlife Sanctuary"), located on the north shore of Lake Walter E. Long, is a park managed by the Austin Parks and Recreation Department with the goal of restoring the [Blackland Prairie](/wiki/Blackland_Prairie "Blackland Prairie"). While not open to the public, it is accessible through guided tours.

As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), Austin had a population of 961,855. The median age was 33.0 years. 19.4% of residents were under the age of 18 and 9.5% of residents were 65 years of age or older. For every 100 females there were 102.0 males, and for every 100 females age 18 and over there were 101.4 males age 18 and over.[[126]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-130)[[127]](#cite_note-Census2020PL-131)

99.5% of residents lived in urban areas, while 0.5% lived in rural areas.[[128]](#cite_note-Census2020DHC-132)

There were 410,868 households in Austin, of which 24.9% had children under the age of 18 living in them. Of all households, 35.0% were married-couple households, 26.5% were households with a male householder and no spouse or partner present, and 29.0% were households with a female householder and no spouse or partner present. About 35.3% of all households were made up of individuals and 6.1% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older.[[126]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-130)

There were 444,426 housing units, of which 7.6% were vacant. Among occupied housing units, 41.2% were owner-occupied and 58.8% were renter-occupied. The homeowner vacancy rate was 1.2% and the rental vacancy rate was 7.8%.[[126]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-130)

The 2000 census found that the median income for a household in the city was [US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")42,689, and the median income for a family was $54,091. Males had a median income of $35,545 compared to $30,046 for females. The per capita income for the city was $24,163. About 9.1% of families and 14.4% of the population were below the poverty line, including 16.5% of those under age 18 and 8.7% of those age 65 or over. The median house price was $185,906 in 2009, and it has increased every year since 2004.[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*][[129]](#cite_note-133) The median value of a house which the owner occupies was $318,400 in 2019—higher than the average American home value of $240,500.[[130]](#cite_note-134)

A 2014 [University of Texas](/wiki/University_of_Texas_at_Austin "University of Texas at Austin") study stated that Austin was the only U.S. city with a fast growth rate between 2000 and 2010 with a net loss in African Americans. As of 2014[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit), Austin's African American and non-Hispanic White percentage shares of the total population were declining despite the total populations of both ethnic groups increasing, as their rate of growth was slower than that of the city's Asian and Hispanic or Latino communities. Austin's non-Hispanic White population dropped below 50% in 2005.[[135]](#cite_note-139)[[136]](#cite_note-140)[[137]](#cite_note-141)

According to the [2010 United States census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census"),[[138]](#cite_note-142) the city of Austin had a population of 790,390. The racial and ethnic composition of Austin was 68.3% [White](/wiki/White_American "White American") (48.7% [non-Hispanic White](/wiki/Non-Hispanic_White "Non-Hispanic White")), 35.1% [Hispanic or Latino](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") (29.1% [Mexican](/wiki/Mexican_Americans "Mexican Americans"), 0.5% [Puerto Rican](/wiki/Puerto_Ricans "Puerto Ricans"), 0.4% [Cuban](/wiki/Cuban_Americans "Cuban Americans"), 5.1% Other), 8.1% [African American](/wiki/African_American "African American"), 6.3% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_American "Asian American") (1.9% [Indian](/wiki/Indian_Americans "Indian Americans"), 1.5% [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_Americans "Chinese Americans"), 1.0% [Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_Americans "Vietnamese Americans"), 0.7% [Korean](/wiki/Korean_Americans "Korean Americans"), 0.3% [Filipino](/wiki/Filipino_Americans "Filipino Americans"), 0.2% [Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_Americans "Japanese Americans"), 0.8% Other), 0.9% [American Indian](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States"), 0.1% [Native Hawaiian and Other Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander_American "Pacific Islander American"), and 3.4% of [two or more races](/wiki/Multiracial_American "Multiracial American"). By 2020, its racial and ethnic composition was 47.1% non-Hispanic white, 32.5% Hispanic or Latino, 8.9% Asian, 6.9% Black or African American, and 3.9% multiracial.

### Sexual orientation and gender identity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Sexual orientation and gender identity")]

According to a survey completed in 2014 by [Gallup](/wiki/Gallup_(company) "Gallup (company)"), it was estimated that 5.3% of residents in the [Austin metropolitan area](/wiki/Greater_Austin "Greater Austin") identified as [lesbian, gay, bisexual, or transgender](/wiki/LGBT "LGBT").[[139]](#cite_note-143) The Austin metropolitan area had the third-highest rate in the nation.[[140]](#cite_note-144)

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Religion")]

According to [Sperling's BestPlaces](/wiki/Sperling%27s_BestPlaces "Sperling's BestPlaces"), 52.4% of Austin's population identified as religious in 2018.[[141]](#cite_note-TXReligion-145) The majority of Austinites identified themselves as [Christians](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"); about 25.2% of the population claimed affiliation with the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church").[[141]](#cite_note-TXReligion-145) The city's Catholic population is served by the [Roman Catholic Diocese of Austin](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Austin "Roman Catholic Diocese of Austin"), headquartered at the [Cathedral of Saint Mary](/wiki/Cathedral_of_Saint_Mary_(Austin,_Texas) "Cathedral of Saint Mary (Austin, Texas)"). Nationwide, 23% of Americans identified as Catholic in 2016.[[142]](#cite_note-146) Other significant Christian groups in Austin include [Baptists](/wiki/Baptists "Baptists") (8.7%), followed by [Methodists](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism") (4.3%), [Latter-day Saints](/wiki/The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints "The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints") (1.5%), [Episcopalians or Anglicans](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism") (1.0%), [Lutherans](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") (0.8%), [Presbyterians](/wiki/Presbyterianism "Presbyterianism") (0.6%), [Pentecostals](/wiki/Pentecostalism "Pentecostalism") (0.3%), and other Christians such as the [Disciples of Christ](/wiki/Christian_Church_(Disciples_of_Christ) "Christian Church (Disciples of Christ)") and [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") (7.1%).[[141]](#cite_note-TXReligion-145) The second largest religion Austinites identify with is [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") (1.7%); roughly 0.8% of Americans nationwide claimed affiliation with the Islamic faith.[[141]](#cite_note-TXReligion-145) The dominant branch of Islam is [Sunni Islam](/wiki/Sunni_Islam "Sunni Islam"). Established in 1977, the largest [mosque](/wiki/Mosque "Mosque") in Austin is the [Islamic Center of Greater Austin](/wiki/Islamic_Center_of_Greater_Austin "Islamic Center of Greater Austin"). The community is affiliated with the [Islamic Society of North America](/wiki/Islamic_Society_of_North_America "Islamic Society of North America"). [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism") forms less than 0.1% of the religious demographic in Austin. [Orthodox](/wiki/Orthodox_Judaism "Orthodox Judaism"), [Reform](/wiki/Reform_Judaism "Reform Judaism"), and [Conservative](/wiki/Conservative_Judaism "Conservative Judaism") congregations are present in the community.[[141]](#cite_note-TXReligion-145) The same study says that [eastern faiths](/wiki/Eastern_religions "Eastern religions") including [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") and [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") made up 0.9% of the city's religious population.[[141]](#cite_note-TXReligion-145) Several Hindu temples exist in the Austin Metropolitan area with the most notable one being [Radha Madhav Dham](/wiki/Radha_Madhav_Dham "Radha Madhav Dham"). In addition to those religious groups, Austin is also home to an active secular humanist community, hosting nationwide television shows and charity work.[[143]](#cite_note-147)

### Homelessness

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Homelessness")]

As of 2019, there were 2,255 individuals experiencing homelessness in Travis County. Of those, 1,169 were sheltered and 1,086 were unsheltered.[[144]](#cite_note-148) In September 2019, the Austin City Council approved $62.7 million for programs aimed at homelessness, which includes housing displacement prevention, crisis mitigation, and [affordable housing](/wiki/Affordable_housing "Affordable housing"); the city council also earmarked $500,000 for crisis services and encampment cleanups.[[145]](#cite_note-149)

In June 2019, following *[Martin v. Boise](/wiki/Martin_v._Boise "Martin v. Boise")*, a federal court ruling on homelessness sleeping in public,[[146]](#cite_note-150) the Austin City Council lifted a 25-year-old ban on camping, sitting, or lying down in public unless doing so causes an obstruction. The resolution also included the approval of a new housing-focused shelter in South Austin.[[147]](#cite_note-151) In early October 2019, Texas Governor [Greg Abbott](/wiki/Greg_Abbott "Greg Abbott") sent a letter to Mayor Steve Adler threatening to deploy state resources to combat the camping ban repeal.[[148]](#cite_note-Bova-152) On October 17, 2019, the City Council revised the camping ordinance, which imposed increased restrictions on sidewalk camping.[[148]](#cite_note-Bova-152) In November 2019, the State of Texas opened a temporary homeless encampment on a former vehicle storage yard owned by the [Texas Department of Transportation](/wiki/Texas_Department_of_Transportation "Texas Department of Transportation").[[149]](#cite_note-153)

In May 2021, the camping ban was reinstated after a [ballot proposition](/wiki/Referendum "Referendum") was approved by 57% of voters. The ban introduces penalties for camping, sitting, or lying down on a public sidewalk or sleeping outdoors in or near Downtown Austin or the area around the [University of Texas](/wiki/University_of_Texas_at_Austin "University of Texas at Austin") campus. The ordinance also prohibits solicitation at certain locations.[[150]](#cite_note-154)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Economy")]

See also: [Silicon Hills](/wiki/Silicon_Hills "Silicon Hills") and [List of companies based in Austin, Texas](/wiki/List_of_companies_based_in_Austin,_Texas "List of companies based in Austin, Texas")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Biking_in_Congress_Avenue.JPG/250px-Biking_in_Congress_Avenue.JPG)](/wiki/File:Biking_in_Congress_Avenue.JPG)

Downtown Austin from Congress Avenue Bridge, with Texas State Capitol in background, 2012

The [Greater Austin](/wiki/Greater_Austin "Greater Austin") [metropolitan statistical area](/wiki/Metropolitan_statistical_area "Metropolitan statistical area") had a gross domestic product (GDP) of $248 billion in 2023.[[151]](#cite_note-155) Austin is considered to be a major center for [high tech](/wiki/High_tech "High tech").[[152]](#cite_note-CityDataEconomy-156) Thousands of graduates each year from the engineering and computer science programs at the [University of Texas at Austin](/wiki/University_of_Texas_at_Austin "University of Texas at Austin") provide a steady source of employees that help to fuel Austin's technology and defense industry sectors. As a result of the high concentration of high-tech companies in the region, Austin was strongly affected by the [dot-com boom](/wiki/Dot-com_bubble "Dot-com bubble") in the late 1990s and subsequent bust.[[152]](#cite_note-CityDataEconomy-156) Austin's largest employers include the [Austin Independent School District](/wiki/Austin_Independent_School_District "Austin Independent School District"), the City of Austin, [Dell Technologies](/wiki/Dell_Technologies "Dell Technologies"), the [U.S. Federal Government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States"), [NXP Semiconductors](/wiki/NXP_Semiconductors "NXP Semiconductors"), [IBM](/wiki/IBM "IBM"), St. David's Healthcare Partnership, Seton Family of Hospitals, the [State of Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas"), the [Texas State University](/wiki/Texas_State_University "Texas State University"), and the [University of Texas at Austin](/wiki/University_of_Texas_at_Austin "University of Texas at Austin").[[152]](#cite_note-CityDataEconomy-156)

Other high-tech companies with operations in Austin include [3M](/wiki/3M "3M"), [Apple](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc.") (the largest campus outside of Cupertino), [Amazon](/wiki/Amazon_(company) "Amazon (company)"), [AMD](/wiki/Advanced_Micro_Devices "Advanced Micro Devices"), Apartment Ratings, [Applied Materials](/wiki/Applied_Materials "Applied Materials"), [Arm](/wiki/Arm_(company) "Arm (company)"), [Bigcommerce](/wiki/Bigcommerce "Bigcommerce"), [BioWare](/wiki/BioWare "BioWare"), [Blizzard Entertainment](/wiki/Blizzard_Entertainment "Blizzard Entertainment"), [Buffalo Technology](/wiki/Buffalo_Technology "Buffalo Technology"), [Cirrus Logic](/wiki/Cirrus_Logic "Cirrus Logic"), [Cisco Systems](/wiki/Cisco_Systems "Cisco Systems"), [Cloudflare](/wiki/Cloudflare "Cloudflare"), [CrowdStrike](/wiki/CrowdStrike "CrowdStrike"), [Dropbox](/wiki/Dropbox_(service) "Dropbox (service)"), [eBay](/wiki/EBay "EBay"), [Electronic Arts](/wiki/Electronic_Arts "Electronic Arts"), [Flextronics](/wiki/Flextronics "Flextronics"), [Facebook](/wiki/Facebook "Facebook"), [Google](/wiki/Google "Google"), [Hewlett-Packard](/wiki/Hewlett-Packard "Hewlett-Packard"), [Hoover's](/wiki/Hoover%27s "Hoover's"), [HomeAway](/wiki/HomeAway "HomeAway"), [HostGator](/wiki/HostGator "HostGator"), [Indeed](/wiki/Indeed "Indeed"), [Intel Corporation](/wiki/Intel_Corporation "Intel Corporation"), [Meta](/wiki/Meta_Platforms "Meta Platforms"), [National Instruments](/wiki/National_Instruments "National Instruments"), [Nintendo](/wiki/Nintendo "Nintendo"), [Nvidia](/wiki/Nvidia "Nvidia"), [Oracle](/wiki/Oracle_Corporation "Oracle Corporation"), [PayPal](/wiki/PayPal "PayPal"), [Polycom](/wiki/Polycom "Polycom"), [Qualcomm](/wiki/Qualcomm,_Inc. "Qualcomm, Inc."), [Rackspace](/wiki/Rackspace "Rackspace"), [RetailMeNot](/wiki/RetailMeNot,_Inc. "RetailMeNot, Inc."), [Rooster Teeth](/wiki/Rooster_Teeth "Rooster Teeth"), [Samsung Group](/wiki/Samsung "Samsung"), [Silicon Labs](/wiki/Silicon_Labs "Silicon Labs"), [Spansion](/wiki/Spansion "Spansion"), [TikTok](/wiki/TikTok "TikTok"), [United Devices](/wiki/United_Devices "United Devices"), [VMware](/wiki/VMware "VMware"), [X (formerly Twitter)](/wiki/X_Corp. "X Corp."), [Xerox](/wiki/Xerox "Xerox"), and [Zoho Corporation](/wiki/Zoho_Corporation "Zoho Corporation"). The proliferation of technology companies has led to the region's nickname, "Silicon Hills", and spurred development that greatly expanded the city.

[Tesla, Inc.](/wiki/Tesla,_Inc. "Tesla, Inc."), an [electric vehicle](/wiki/Electric_vehicle "Electric vehicle") and [electric power](/wiki/Electric_power "Electric power") company has its corporate headquarters in Austin inside [Gigafactory Texas](/wiki/Gigafactory_Texas "Gigafactory Texas"), a large vehicle assembly plant which employs over 20,000 people. The company expects to eventually have a staff of 60,000 in the Austin area as production ramps up.[[153]](#cite_note-Hardison_2023-157)[*[unreliable source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources")*]

Austin is also emerging as a hub for [pharmaceutical](/wiki/Pharmaceutical_industry "Pharmaceutical industry") and [biotechnology](/wiki/Biotechnology "Biotechnology") companies; the city is home to about 85 of them.[[152]](#cite_note-CityDataEconomy-156) In 2004, the city was ranked by the Milken Institute as the No. 12 biotech and life science center in the United States[[154]](#cite_note-158) and in 2018, [CBRE Group](/wiki/CBRE_Group "CBRE Group") ranked Austin as #3 emerging life sciences cluster.[[155]](#cite_note-159) Companies such as [Hospira](/wiki/Hospira "Hospira"), [Pharmaceutical Product Development](/wiki/Pharmaceutical_Product_Development "Pharmaceutical Product Development"), and [ArthroCare Corporation](/wiki/ArthroCare "ArthroCare") are located there.

[Whole Foods Market](/wiki/Whole_Foods_Market "Whole Foods Market"), an international grocery store chain specializing in fresh and packaged food products, was founded and is headquartered in Austin.[[156]](#cite_note-160)

Other companies based in Austin include [NXP Semiconductors](/wiki/NXP_Semiconductors "NXP Semiconductors"), [GoodPop](/wiki/GoodPop "GoodPop"), [Temple-Inland](/wiki/Temple-Inland "Temple-Inland"), [Sweet Leaf Tea Company](/wiki/Sweet_Leaf_Tea_Company "Sweet Leaf Tea Company"), [Keller Williams Realty](/wiki/Keller_Williams_Realty "Keller Williams Realty"), [National Western Life](/wiki/National_Western_Life "National Western Life"), [GSD&M](/wiki/GSD%26M "GSD&M"), [Dimensional Fund Advisors](/wiki/Dimensional_Fund_Advisors "Dimensional Fund Advisors"), [Golfsmith](/wiki/Golfsmith "Golfsmith"), [Forestar Group](/wiki/Forestar_Group "Forestar Group"), [EZCorp](/wiki/EZCorp "EZCorp"), [Outdoor Voices](/wiki/Outdoor_Voices "Outdoor Voices"), [Tito's Vodka](/wiki/Tito%27s_Vodka "Tito's Vodka"), Speak Social, and [YETI](/wiki/YETI_(company) "YETI (company)").

In 2018, Austin metro-area companies saw a total of $1.33 billion invested. In 2018, Austin's venture capital investments accounted for more than 60 percent of Texas' total investments.[[157]](#cite_note-161)
