## Infrastructure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Infrastructure")]

### Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Transportation")]

In 2009, 72.7% of Austin (city) commuters drove alone, with other [mode shares](/wiki/Mode_share "Mode share") being: 10.4% carpool, 6% were [remote workers](/wiki/Remote_work "Remote work"), 5% use transit, 2.3% walk, and 1% bicycle.[[159]](#cite_note-163)[[160]](#cite_note-164) In 2016, the [American Community Survey](/wiki/American_Community_Survey "American Community Survey") estimated modal shares for Austin (city) commuters of 73.5% for driving alone, 9.6% for carpooling, 3.6% for riding transit, 2% for walking, and 1.5% for cycling.[[161]](#cite_note-165) The city of Austin has a lower than average percentage of households without a car. In 2015, 6.9 percent of Austin households lacked a car, and decreased slightly to 6 percent in 2016. The national average was 8.7 percent in 2016. Austin averaged 1.65 cars per household in 2016, compared to a national average of 1.8.[[162]](#cite_note-166)

#### Highways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Highways")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Pennybacker_bridge_sunset_2006.jpg/250px-Pennybacker_bridge_sunset_2006.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pennybacker_bridge_sunset_2006.jpg)

The [Pennybacker Bridge](/wiki/Pennybacker_Bridge "Pennybacker Bridge") is the signature element of [Loop 360](/wiki/Texas_Loop_360 "Texas Loop 360") in the [Texas Hill Country](/wiki/Texas_Hill_Country "Texas Hill Country").

Central Austin lies between two major north–south freeways: [I-35](/wiki/Interstate_35_in_Texas "Interstate 35 in Texas") to the east and the [Mopac Expressway (Loop 1)](/wiki/Texas_State_Highway_Loop_1 "Texas State Highway Loop 1") to the west. [US 183](/wiki/U.S._Route_183_in_Texas "U.S. Route 183 in Texas") runs from northwest to southeast, and [SH 71](/wiki/Texas_State_Highway_71 "Texas State Highway 71") crosses the southern part of the city from east to west, completing a rough "box" around central and north-central Austin. Austin is the largest city in the United States to be served by only one Interstate Highway.

[US 290](/wiki/U.S._Route_290 "U.S. Route 290") enters Austin from the east and merges into I-35. Its highway designation continues south on I-35 and then becomes part of SH 71, continuing to the west. US 290 splits from Highway 71 in southwest Austin, in an interchange known as "The Y." SH 71 continues to [Brady](/wiki/Brady,_Texas "Brady, Texas"), and Highway 290 continues west to interchange for [I-10](/wiki/Interstate_10_in_Texas "Interstate 10 in Texas") near [Junction](/wiki/Junction,_Texas "Junction, Texas"). I-35 continues south through [San Antonio](/wiki/San_Antonio "San Antonio") to [Laredo](/wiki/Laredo,_Texas "Laredo, Texas") on the Mexican border. I-35 is the highway link to the Dallas-Fort Worth metroplex in Northern Texas. There are two links to Houston (US 290 and SH 71/I-10). US 183 leads northwest of Austin toward [Lampasas](/wiki/Lampasas,_Texas "Lampasas, Texas").

In the mid-1980s, construction was completed on [Loop 360](/wiki/Texas_State_Highway_Loop_360 "Texas State Highway Loop 360"), a scenic highway that curves through the hill country from near the 71/Mopac interchange in the south to near the US 183/Mopac interchange in the north. The iconic [Pennybacker Bridge](/wiki/Pennybacker_Bridge "Pennybacker Bridge"), also known as the "360 Bridge," crosses Lake Austin to connect the northern and southern portions of Loop 360.

#### Tollways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Tollways")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Texas45.jpg/250px-Texas45.jpg)](/wiki/File:Texas45.jpg)

Interchange of [Interstate 35](/wiki/Interstate_35 "Interstate 35") and [State Highway 45](/wiki/State_Highway_45_(Texas) "State Highway 45 (Texas)")

[SH 130](/wiki/Texas_State_Highway_130 "Texas State Highway 130") is a bypass route designed to relieve [traffic congestion](/wiki/Traffic_congestion "Traffic congestion"), starting from I-35 just north of Georgetown and running along a parallel route to the east, where it bypasses [Round Rock](/wiki/Round_Rock,_Texas "Round Rock, Texas"), Austin, [San Marcos](/wiki/San_Marcos,_Texas "San Marcos, Texas") and [New Braunfels](/wiki/New_Braunfels,_Texas "New Braunfels, Texas") before ending at [I-10](/wiki/Interstate_10_in_Texas "Interstate 10 in Texas") east of [Seguin](/wiki/Seguin,_Texas "Seguin, Texas"), where drivers could drive 30 miles (48 km) west to return to I-35 in [San Antonio](/wiki/San_Antonio "San Antonio"). The first segment was opened in November 2006, which was located east of [Austin–Bergstrom International Airport](/wiki/Austin%E2%80%93Bergstrom_International_Airport "Austin–Bergstrom International Airport") at Austin's southeast corner on [SH 71](/wiki/Texas_State_Highway_71 "Texas State Highway 71"). Highway 130 runs concurrently with SH 45 from [Pflugerville](/wiki/Pflugerville,_Texas "Pflugerville, Texas") on the north until it reaches [US 183](/wiki/U.S._Route_183_in_Texas "U.S. Route 183 in Texas") well south of Austin, at which point [SR 45](/wiki/Texas_State_Highway_45 "Texas State Highway 45") continues west. The entire route of SH 130 is now complete. The final leg opened on November 1, 2012. The highway is noted for having a maximum speed limit of 85 mph (137 km/h) for the entire route. The 41-mile (66 km) section of the toll road between Mustang Ridge and Seguin has a posted speed limit of 85 mph (137 km/h), the highest posted [speed limit in the United States](/wiki/Speed_limits_in_the_United_States "Speed limits in the United States").

[SH 45](/wiki/Texas_State_Highway_45 "Texas State Highway 45") runs east–west from just south of US 183 in [Cedar Park](/wiki/Cedar_Park,_Texas "Cedar Park, Texas") to 130 inside [Pflugerville](/wiki/Pflugerville,_Texas "Pflugerville, Texas") (just east of Round Rock). A tolled extension of State Highway Loop 1 was also created. A new southeast leg of SH 45 has recently been completed, running from US 183 and the south end of Segment 5 of TX-130 south of Austin due west to I-35 at the [FM 1327](/wiki/List_of_Farm_to_Market_Roads_in_Texas#FM_1327 "List of Farm to Market Roads in Texas")/Creedmoor Road exit between the south end of Austin and [Buda](/wiki/Buda,_Texas "Buda, Texas"). The 183A Toll Road opened in March 2007, providing a tolled alternative to US 183 through the cities of [Leander](/wiki/Leander,_Texas "Leander, Texas") and [Cedar Park](/wiki/Cedar_Park,_Texas "Cedar Park, Texas"). Currently under construction is a change to East US 290 from US 183 to the town of Manor. Officially, the tollway will be dubbed Tollway 290 with "Manor Expressway" as nickname.

Despite the overwhelming initial opposition to the toll road concept when it was first announced, all three toll roads have exceeded revenue projections.[[163]](#cite_note-Wear_2009-167)

#### Airports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Airports")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/AUSCentralConcourse.JPG/250px-AUSCentralConcourse.JPG)](/wiki/File:AUSCentralConcourse.JPG)

The Barbara Jordan Terminal at [Austin–Bergstrom International Airport](/wiki/Austin%E2%80%93Bergstrom_International_Airport "Austin–Bergstrom International Airport")

Austin's primary airport is [Austin–Bergstrom International Airport](/wiki/Austin%E2%80%93Bergstrom_International_Airport "Austin–Bergstrom International Airport") (ABIA) ([IATA code](/wiki/List_of_airports_by_IATA_code:_A#AU "List of airports by IATA code: A") **AUS**), located 5 miles (8 km) southeast of the city. The airport is on the site of the former [Bergstrom Air Force Base](/wiki/Bergstrom_Air_Force_Base "Bergstrom Air Force Base"), which was closed in 1993 as part of the [Base Realignment and Closure](/wiki/Base_Realignment_and_Closure "Base Realignment and Closure") process. Until 1999, [Robert Mueller Municipal Airport](/wiki/Robert_Mueller_Municipal_Airport "Robert Mueller Municipal Airport") was Austin's main airport until ABIA took that role and the old airport was shut down.[[164]](#cite_note-LA_Times_Closed-168) [Austin Executive Airport](/wiki/Austin_Executive_Airport "Austin Executive Airport"), along with several smaller airports outside the city center, serves general aviation traffic.

#### Intercity transit

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Intercity transit")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Texas_Eagle_arriving_in_Austin%2C_en_route_from_Los_Angeles_to_Chicago.jpg/250px-Texas_Eagle_arriving_in_Austin%2C_en_route_from_Los_Angeles_to_Chicago.jpg)](/wiki/File:Texas_Eagle_arriving_in_Austin,_en_route_from_Los_Angeles_to_Chicago.jpg)

Amtrak's *Texas Eagle* stops in Austin twice daily.

[Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak")'s [Austin Station](/wiki/Austin_station_(Texas) "Austin station (Texas)") is located in west downtown and is served by the *[Texas Eagle](/wiki/Texas_Eagle "Texas Eagle")* which runs daily between Chicago and San Antonio, continuing on to Los Angeles several times a week.[[165]](#cite_note-Amtrak_Texas_Eagle-169)

Railway segments between Austin and San Antonio have been evaluated for a proposed regional passenger rail project called "Lone Star Rail". However, failure to come to an agreement with the track's current owner, [Union Pacific Railroad](/wiki/Union_Pacific_Railroad "Union Pacific Railroad"), ended the project in 2016.[[166]](#cite_note-Maas_2016-170)

[Greyhound Lines](/wiki/Greyhound_Lines "Greyhound Lines") operates the current Austin Bus Station at the Eastside Bus Plaza[[167]](#cite_note-171) Grupo Senda's Turimex Internacional service operates bus service from Austin to Nuevo Laredo and on to many destinations in Mexico from their station in East Austin.[[168]](#cite_note-Turimex-172) [Megabus](/wiki/Megabus_(North_America) "Megabus (North America)") offers daily service to San Antonio, Dallas/Fort Worth and Houston.[[169]](#cite_note-Megabus_PR-173)

#### Public transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Public transportation")]

See also: [CapMetro](/wiki/CapMetro "CapMetro")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Austin_Metrorail.jpg/250px-Austin_Metrorail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Austin_Metrorail.jpg)

[CapMetro Rail](/wiki/CapMetro_Rail "CapMetro Rail") train at [Downtown station](/wiki/Downtown_station_(CapMetro_Rail) "Downtown station (CapMetro Rail)")

The Capital Metropolitan Transportation Authority ([CapMetro](/wiki/CapMetro "CapMetro")) provides public transportation to the city, primarily with its [CapMetro Bus](/wiki/CapMetro_Bus "CapMetro Bus") local bus service, the [CapMetro Express](/wiki/CapMetro_Bus#CapMetro_Express "CapMetro Bus") express bus system, as well as a [bus rapid transit](/wiki/Bus_rapid_transit "Bus rapid transit") service, [CapMetro Rapid](/wiki/CapMetro_Rapid "CapMetro Rapid"). CapMetro opened a 32-mile (51 km) [hybrid rail](/wiki/Light_rail_in_the_United_States#"Hybrid_rail_systems" "Light rail in the United States") system, [CapMetro Rail](/wiki/CapMetro_Rail "CapMetro Rail"), in 2010.[[170]](#cite_note-Cap_Metro_All_Systems_Go_2009-174) The system consists of a single line serving downtown Austin, the neighborhoods of East Austin, [North Central Austin](/wiki/North_Central_Austin "North Central Austin"), and Northwest Austin plus the suburb of [Leander](/wiki/Leander,_Texas "Leander, Texas").

Since it began operations in 1985, CapMetro has proposed adding [light rail](/wiki/Light_rail "Light rail") services to its network. Despite support from the City Council, voters rejected light rail proposals in 2000[[171]](#cite_note-Clark-Madison_2000-175) and 2014.[[172]](#cite_note-Curington_2014-176) However, in 2020, voters approved CapMetro's US$10 billion transit expansion plan, [Project Connect](/wiki/Project_Connect "Project Connect"), by a comfortable margin. The plan proposes 2 new light rail lines, an additional bus rapid transit line (which could be converted to light rail in the future), a second commuter rail line, several new MetroRapid lines, more MetroExpress routes, and a number of other infrastructure, technology and service expansion projects.[[173]](#cite_note-Cap_Metro_Project_Connect-177)

[Capital Area Rural Transportation System](/wiki/Capital_Area_Rural_Transportation_System "Capital Area Rural Transportation System") connects Austin with outlying suburbs and surrounding rural areas.

#### Ride sharing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Ride sharing")]

Austin is served by several [ride-sharing](/wiki/Ridesharing_company "Ridesharing company") companies including [Uber](/wiki/Uber "Uber") and [Lyft](/wiki/Lyft "Lyft").[[174]](#cite_note-Uber_Austin-178)[[175]](#cite_note-Lyft_Austin-179)[[176]](#cite_note-RideAustin-180) On May 9, 2016, Uber and Lyft voluntarily ceased operations in Austin in response to a city ordinance that required ride sharing company drivers to get fingerprint checks, have their vehicles labeled, and not pick up or drop off in certain city lanes.[[177]](#cite_note-MacMillan_2016-181)[[178]](#cite_note-Woodyard_2016-182) Uber and Lyft resumed service in the summer of 2017.[[179]](#cite_note-Samuels_2017-183) The city was previously served by [Fasten](/wiki/Fasten_(company) "Fasten (company)") until they ceased all operations in the city in March 2018.[[180]](#cite_note-Jechow_2018-184)

Austin is also served by Electric Cab of North America's six-passenger [electric cabs](/wiki/Electric_car "Electric car") that operate on a flexible route from the [Kramer MetroRail Station](/wiki/Kramer_station "Kramer station") to [Domain Northside](/wiki/The_Domain_(Austin,_Texas) "The Domain (Austin, Texas)") and from the [Downtown CapMetro Rail station](/wiki/Downtown_station_(CapMetro_Rail) "Downtown station (CapMetro Rail)") and MetroRapid stops to locations between the [Austin Convention Center](/wiki/Austin_Convention_Center "Austin Convention Center") and near Sixth and Bowie streets by [Whole Foods](/wiki/Whole_Foods_Market "Whole Foods Market").[[181]](#cite_note-Denney_2018-185)

[Carsharing](/wiki/Carsharing "Carsharing") service [Zipcar](/wiki/Zipcar "Zipcar") operates in Austin and, until 2019, the city was also served by [Car2Go](/wiki/Car2Go "Car2Go") which kept its North American headquarters in the city even after pulling out.[[182]](#cite_note-Turner_2019-186)

#### Cycling and walking

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Cycling and walking")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Pfluger_Pedestrian_Bridge.jpg/330px-Pfluger_Pedestrian_Bridge.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pfluger_Pedestrian_Bridge.jpg)

The Pfluger Pedestrian Bridge over the Colorado River

The city's bike advocacy organization is Bike Austin.[[183]](#cite_note-187) BikeTexas, a state-level advocacy organization, also has its main office in Austin.[[184]](#cite_note-188)

[Bicycles](/wiki/Bicycle "Bicycle") are a popular transportation choice among students, faculty, and staff at the University of Texas. According to a survey done at the University of Texas, 57% of commuters bike to campus.[[185]](#cite_note-189)

The City of Austin and CapMetro jointly own a [bike-sharing](/wiki/Bike-sharing "Bike-sharing") service, [CapMetro Bike](/wiki/CapMetro#Other_services "CapMetro"), which is available in and around downtown. The service is a franchise of [BCycle](/wiki/BCycle "BCycle"), a national bike sharing network owned by [Trek Bicycle](/wiki/Trek_Bicycle "Trek Bicycle"), and is operated by local nonprofit organization Bike Share of Austin.[[186]](#cite_note-190) Until 2020 the service was known as Austin BCycle.[[187]](#cite_note-191) In 2018, [Lime](/wiki/LimeBike "LimeBike") began offering [dockless bikes](/wiki/Dockless_bikes "Dockless bikes"), which do not need to be docked at a designated station.[[188]](#cite_note-192)

In 2018, [scooter-sharing](/wiki/Scooter-sharing_system "Scooter-sharing system") companies [Lime](/wiki/Lime_(transportation_company) "Lime (transportation company)") and [Bird](/wiki/Bird_Global "Bird Global") debuted [rentable electric scooters](/wiki/Scooter-sharing_system "Scooter-sharing system") in Austin. The city briefly banned the scooters — which began operations before the city could implement a permitting system — until the city completed development of their "dockless mobility" permitting process on May 1, 2018.[[189]](#cite_note-193) Dockless electric scooters and bikes are banned from Austin city parks and the [Ann and Roy Butler Trail and Boardwalk](/wiki/Lady_Bird_Lake#Ann_and_Roy_Butler_Hike-and-Bike_Trail_and_Boardwalk "Lady Bird Lake").[[190]](#cite_note-194) For the 2018 [Austin City Limits Music Festival](/wiki/Austin_City_Limits_Music_Festival "Austin City Limits Music Festival"), the city of Austin offered a designated parking area for dockless bikes and scooters.[[191]](#cite_note-195)

#### Parking lots

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Parking lots")]

In November 2023, Austin became the largest city in the US that has abolished [parking mandates](/wiki/Parking_mandates "Parking mandates"). It did so to encourage walking, biking, and public transit use, as well as to lower the cost of housing and increase the amount of housing units that can be built in the city. [Portland](/wiki/Portland,_Oregon "Portland, Oregon") and [Minneapolis](/wiki/Minneapolis "Minneapolis") also took this action.[[192]](#cite_note-196)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Museum_weird_2012.jpg/250px-Museum_weird_2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:Museum_weird_2012.jpg)

Museum of the Weird on Sixth Street

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Mexican_American_Cultural_Center%2C_Austin%2C_TX_IMG_6232.JPG/250px-Mexican_American_Cultural_Center%2C_Austin%2C_TX_IMG_6232.JPG)](/wiki/File:Mexican_American_Cultural_Center,_Austin,_TX_IMG_6232.JPG)

The Emma S. Barrientos Mexican American Cultural Center, located on Lady Bird Lake at 600 River Street

"Keep Austin Weird" has been a local [motto](/wiki/Motto "Motto") for years, featured on bumper stickers and T-shirts. This motto has not only been used in promoting Austin's eccentricity and diversity, but is also meant to bolster support of local independent businesses.[[20]](#cite_note-weird-21) According to the 2010 book *[Weird City](/wiki/Weird_City "Weird City")* the phrase was begun by a local Austin Community College librarian, Red Wassenich, and his wife, Karen Pavelka, who were concerned about Austin's "rapid descent into commercialism and overdevelopment."[[16]](#cite_note-Long_2010-17) The slogan has been interpreted many ways since its inception, but remains an important symbol for many Austinites who wish to voice concerns over rapid growth and development. Austin has a long history of vocal citizen resistance to development projects perceived to degrade the environment, or to threaten the natural and cultural landscapes.[[193]](#cite_note-Swearingen_2010-197)

According to the [Nielsen Company](/wiki/Nielsen_Company "Nielsen Company"), adults in Austin read and contribute to blogs more than those in any other U.S. metropolitan area and have the highest Internet usage in all of Texas.[[194]](#cite_note-Nielsen_Company-198) In 2013, Austin was the most active city on [Reddit](/wiki/Reddit "Reddit"), having the largest number of views per capita.[[195]](#cite_note-Dunbar_2013-199)

[South Congress](/wiki/South_Congress "South Congress") is a shopping district stretching down South Congress Avenue from Downtown. This area is home to coffee shops, eccentric stores, restaurants, food trucks, trailers, and festivals. It prides itself on "Keeping Austin Weird," especially with development in the surrounding area(s). Many Austinites attribute its enduring popularity to the unobstructed [view](/wiki/Texas_Capitol_View_Corridors "Texas Capitol View Corridors") of the Texas State Capitol.[[34]](#cite_note-Patoski_2010-35)

The [Rainey Street Historic District](/wiki/Rainey_Street_Historic_District "Rainey Street Historic District") is a neighborhood in Downtown Austin formerly consisting of [bungalow](/wiki/Bungalow "Bungalow") style homes built in the early 20th century. Since the early 2010s, the former [working class](/wiki/Working_class "Working class") residential street has turned into a popular nightlife district. Much of the historic homes have been renovated into hotels, condominiums, bars and restaurants, many of which feature large porches and outdoor yards for patrons.[[196]](#cite_note-200) The Rainey Street district is also home to the Emma S. Barrientos Mexican American Cultural Center.

Austin has been part of the [UNESCO Creative Cities Network](/wiki/Creative_Cities_Network "Creative Cities Network") under the category [Media Arts](/wiki/Media_arts "Media arts").[[197]](#cite_note-201)

### Old Austin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Old Austin")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Austin_Texas_Rent.webp/250px-Austin_Texas_Rent.webp.png)](/wiki/File:Austin_Texas_Rent.webp)

Austin, Texas average monthly rent

"Old Austin" is an [adage](/wiki/Adage "Adage") often used by [nostalgic](/wiki/Nostalgic "Nostalgic") natives.[[198]](#cite_note-202) The term "Old Austin" refers to a time when the city was smaller and more [bohemian](/wiki/Bohemianism "Bohemianism") with a considerably lower cost of living and better known for its lack of traffic, [hipsters](/wiki/Hipster_(contemporary_subculture) "Hipster (contemporary subculture)"), and [urban sprawl](/wiki/Urban_sprawl "Urban sprawl").[[199]](#cite_note-203) It is often employed by longtime residents expressing displeasure at the rapidly changing culture,[[200]](#cite_note-204) or when referencing nostalgia of Austin culture.[[201]](#cite_note-205)

The growth and popularity of Austin[[202]](#cite_note-206) can be seen by the expansive development taking place in its downtown landscape.[[203]](#cite_note-207) This growth can have a negative impact on longtime small businesses that cannot keep up with the expenses associated with [gentrification](/wiki/Gentrification "Gentrification") and the rising cost of real estate.[[204]](#cite_note-208) A former Austin musician, [Dale Watson](/wiki/Dale_Watson_(singer) "Dale Watson (singer)"), described his move away from Austin, "I just really feel the city has sold itself. Just because you're going to get $45 million for a company to come to town – if it's not in the best interest of the town, I don't think they should do it. This city was never about money. It was about quality of life."[[205]](#cite_note-209) Though much is changing rapidly in Austin, businesses such as Thundercloud Subs are thought by many to maintain classic Austin business cultural sentiments unique to the history of the city; as Diana Burgess stated, "I definitely appreciate that they haven't raised their prices a ton or made things super fancy. I think it speaks to that original Old Austin vibe. A lot of us that grew up here really appreciate that."[[206]](#cite_note-210) Aaron Franklin, owner of [Franklin Barbecue](/wiki/Franklin_Barbecue "Franklin Barbecue"), credited the Old Austin cultural mindset and community support with the success of his barbecue restaurant and the long lines that have supported his business since starting it out of a food trailer in 2009.[[207]](#cite_note-211)

### Annual cultural events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Annual cultural events")]

See also: [Category:Festivals in Austin, Texas](/wiki/Category:Festivals_in_Austin,_Texas "Category:Festivals in Austin, Texas")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Sixth_Street_%28Austin%29_at_night.jpg/250px-Sixth_Street_%28Austin%29_at_night.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sixth_Street_(Austin)_at_night.jpg)

[Sixth Street](/wiki/Sixth_Street_(Austin,_Texas) "Sixth Street (Austin, Texas)") on a weekend night

The [O. Henry House](/wiki/William_Sidney_Porter_House "William Sidney Porter House") Museum hosts the annual [O. Henry Pun-Off](/wiki/O._Henry_Pun-Off "O. Henry Pun-Off"), a pun contest where the successful contestants exhibit wit akin to that of the author [William Sydney Porter](/wiki/William_Sydney_Porter "William Sydney Porter").

Other annual events include [Eeyore's Birthday Party](/wiki/Eeyore%27s_Birthday_Party "Eeyore's Birthday Party"), [Spamarama](/wiki/Spamarama "Spamarama"), Austin [Pride](/wiki/LGBT_pride "LGBT pride") Festival & Parade in August, the Austin Reggae Festival in April,[[208]](#cite_note-212) Kite Festival, Texas Craft Brewers Festival in September,[[209]](#cite_note-213) Art City Austin in April,[[210]](#cite_note-214) East Austin Studio Tour in November,[[211]](#cite_note-215) and [Carnaval Brasileiro](/wiki/Carnaval_Brasileiro_in_Austin,_Texas "Carnaval Brasileiro in Austin, Texas") in February. Sixth Street features annual festivals such as the [Pecan Street Festival](/wiki/Pecan_Street_Festival "Pecan Street Festival") and Halloween night. The three-day [Austin City Limits Music Festival](/wiki/Austin_City_Limits_Music_Festival "Austin City Limits Music Festival") has been held in [Zilker Park](/wiki/Zilker_Park "Zilker Park") every year since 2002. Every year around the end of March and the beginning of April, Austin is home to "Texas Relay Weekend."

Austin's Zilker Park Tree is a Christmas display made of lights strung from the top of a [Moonlight tower](/wiki/Moonlight_tower "Moonlight tower") in Zilker Park. The Zilker Tree is lit in December along with the "Trail of Lights," an Austin Christmas tradition. The Trail of Lights was canceled four times, first starting in 2001 and 2002 due to the September 11 Attacks, and again in 2010 and 2011 due to budget shortfalls, but the trail was turned back on for the 2012 holiday season.[[212]](#cite_note-216)

From 1962 to 1998, the [Austin Aqua Festival](/wiki/Austin_Aqua_Festival "Austin Aqua Festival"), or "Aqua Fest", took place on the shores of Town Lake (now known as [Lady Bird Lake](/wiki/Lady_Bird_Lake "Lady Bird Lake")). Originally conceived as a summer tourism draw, the multi-day event evolved from water-themed activities to a broader civic festival due to growth and community interest. Eventually attendance and financial solvency began to dwindle as larger music and summer festivals grew in prominence.

### Cuisine and breweries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Cuisine and breweries")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Two_of_many_food_trucks_and_trailers_that_serve_the_eclectic_South_Austin_neighborhood_of_Austin%2C_Texas_LCCN2014632540.tif/lossy-page1-250px-Two_of_many_food_trucks_and_trailers_that_serve_the_eclectic_South_Austin_neighborhood_of_Austin%2C_Texas_LCCN2014632540.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:Two_of_many_food_trucks_and_trailers_that_serve_the_eclectic_South_Austin_neighborhood_of_Austin,_Texas_LCCN2014632540.tif)

A food truck trailer park in South Austin

Notable Austin cuisine includes [Texas barbecue](/wiki/Texas_barbecue "Texas barbecue") and [Tex-Mex](/wiki/Tex-Mex "Tex-Mex"); [Franklin Barbecue](/wiki/Franklin_Barbecue "Franklin Barbecue") in Austin's has sold out of [brisket](/wiki/Brisket "Brisket") every day since its establishment.[[213]](#cite_note-217)[[214]](#cite_note-218) [Breakfast tacos](/wiki/Breakfast_taco "Breakfast taco") and [queso](/wiki/Chili_con_queso "Chili con queso") are popular food items in the city; Austin is sometimes called the "home of the breakfast taco."[[215]](#cite_note-219) [Kolaches](/wiki/Kolach_(cake) "Kolach (cake)") are a common pastry in Austin bakeries due to the large [Czech](/wiki/Czech_Americans "Czech Americans") and [German](/wiki/German_Americans "German Americans") immigrant population in Texas.[[216]](#cite_note-220) The [Oasis Restaurant](/wiki/Oasis_Restaurant "Oasis Restaurant") is the largest outdoor restaurant in Texas, which promotes itself as the "Sunset Capital of Texas" with its terraced views looking West over [Lake Travis](/wiki/Lake_Travis "Lake Travis").[[217]](#cite_note-221) [Birdie's](/wiki/Birdie%27s "Birdie's"), a [counter-service](/wiki/Counter_service "Counter service") restaurant and wine bar that opened in 2021, was *Food & Wine*'s 2023 Restaurant of the Year.[[218]](#cite_note-:0-222)[[219]](#cite_note-:6-223)[[220]](#cite_note-:3-224)

[P. Terry's](/wiki/P._Terry%27s "P. Terry's"), an Austin-based fast food burger chain, has a loyal following among Austinites.[[221]](#cite_note-225) Some other Austin-based chain restaurants include [Amy's Ice Creams](/wiki/Amy%27s_Ice_Creams "Amy's Ice Creams"), [Chuy's](/wiki/Chuy%27s "Chuy's"), [DoubleDave's Pizzaworks](/wiki/DoubleDave%27s_Pizzaworks "DoubleDave's Pizzaworks"), and [Schlotzky's](/wiki/Schlotzky%27s "Schlotzky's"). The [Chili's at 45th and Lamar](/wiki/Chili%27s_at_45th_and_Lamar "Chili's at 45th and Lamar") has been the subject of internet memes since 2011.[[222]](#cite_note-226)[[223]](#cite_note-statesman-227)[[224]](#cite_note-228)[[225]](#cite_note-229)

Austin is also home to a large number of [food trucks](/wiki/Food_trucks "Food trucks"), with 1,256 food trucks operating in 2016.[[226]](#cite_note-FoodTrucks-230) The city of Austin has the second-largest number of food trucks per capita in the United States.[[226]](#cite_note-FoodTrucks-230) Austin's first [food hall](/wiki/Food_hall "Food hall"), "Fareground," features a number of Austin-based food vendors and a bar in the ground level and courtyard of [One Congress Plaza](/wiki/One_Congress_Plaza "One Congress Plaza").[[227]](#cite_note-231)

Austin has a large [craft beer](/wiki/Craft_beer "Craft beer") scene, with over 50 [microbreweries](/wiki/Microbreweries "Microbreweries") in the metro area.[[228]](#cite_note-232) Notable Austin-area breweries include [Jester King Brewery](/wiki/Jester_King_Brewery "Jester King Brewery"), [Live Oak Brewing Company](/wiki/Live_Oak_Brewing_Company "Live Oak Brewing Company"), and [Real Ale Brewing Company](/wiki/Real_Ale_Brewing_Company "Real Ale Brewing Company").

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Music")]

Main article: [Music of Austin](/wiki/Music_of_Austin "Music of Austin")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/ACL2009SBH.jpg/250px-ACL2009SBH.jpg)](/wiki/File:ACL2009SBH.jpg)

2009 [Austin City Limits Music Festival](/wiki/Austin_City_Limits_Music_Festival "Austin City Limits Music Festival") with view of stages and Downtown Austin

As Austin's official slogan is *The Live Music Capital of the World*, the city has a vibrant live [music scene](/wiki/Music_of_Austin "Music of Austin") with more music venues per capita than any other U.S. city.[[17]](#cite_note-motto-18)[[18]](#cite_note-atx_motto-19) Austin's music revolves around the many [nightclubs](/wiki/Nightclub "Nightclub") on 6th Street and an annual music/film/[interactive](/wiki/Interactive "Interactive") festival known as [South by Southwest](/wiki/South_by_Southwest "South by Southwest") (SXSW). The concentration of restaurants, bars, and music venues in the city's downtown core is a major contributor to Austin's live music scene, as the ZIP Code encompassing the downtown entertainment district hosts the most bar or alcohol-serving establishments in the U.S.[[229]](#cite_note-233)

The longest-running concert music program on American television, *[Austin City Limits](/wiki/Austin_City_Limits "Austin City Limits")*, is recorded at [ACL Live at The Moody Theater](/wiki/ACL_Live_at_The_Moody_Theater "ACL Live at The Moody Theater"), located in the bottom floor of the 478 feet (146 m) [W Hotels](/wiki/W_Hotels "W Hotels") in Austin.[[230]](#cite_note-234)[[231]](#cite_note-235) *Austin City Limits* and [C3 Presents](/wiki/C3_Presents "C3 Presents") produce the [Austin City Limits Music Festival](/wiki/Austin_City_Limits_Music_Festival "Austin City Limits Music Festival"), an annual music and art festival held at [Zilker Park](/wiki/Zilker_Park "Zilker Park") in Austin. Other music events include the Urban Music Festival, [Fun Fun Fun Fest](/wiki/Fun_Fun_Fun_Fest "Fun Fun Fun Fest"), [Wobeon Music Festival](/wiki/Wobeon_Music_Festival "Wobeon Music Festival"), Chaos In Tejas, Seismic Music Festival at the Concourse Project, and [Old Settler's Music Festival](/wiki/Old_Settler%27s_Music_Festival "Old Settler's Music Festival"). [Austin Lyric Opera](/wiki/Austin_Lyric_Opera "Austin Lyric Opera") performs multiple operas each year (including the 2007 opening of [Philip Glass](/wiki/Philip_Glass "Philip Glass")'s *[Waiting for the Barbarians](/wiki/Waiting_for_the_Barbarians_(opera) "Waiting for the Barbarians (opera)")*, written by University of Texas at Austin alumnus [J. M. Coetzee](/wiki/J._M._Coetzee "J. M. Coetzee")).[[232]](#cite_note-Rossie_2009-236) The [Austin Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Austin_Symphony_Orchestra "Austin Symphony Orchestra") performs a range of classical, pop and family performances and is led by music director and conductor [Peter Bay](/wiki/Peter_Bay "Peter Bay"). The Austin Baroque Orchestra and La Follia Austin Baroque ensembles both give historically informed performances of [Baroque music](/wiki/Baroque_music "Baroque music"). The [Texas Early Music Project](/wiki/Texas_Early_Music_Project "Texas Early Music Project") regularly performs music from the [Medieval](/wiki/Medieval_music "Medieval music") and [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance_music "Renaissance music") eras, as well as the Baroque.

### Film

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Film")]

Austin hosts several film festivals, including the [SXSW (South by Southwest) Film Festival](/wiki/South_by_Southwest_Film_Conference "South by Southwest Film Conference") and the [Austin Film Festival](/wiki/Austin_Film_Festival "Austin Film Festival"), which hosts international films. A [movie theater](/wiki/Movie_theater "Movie theater") chain by the name of [Alamo Drafthouse Cinema](/wiki/Alamo_Drafthouse_Cinema "Alamo Drafthouse Cinema") was founded in Austin in 1997; the South Lamar location of which is home to the annual week-long [Fantastic Fest](/wiki/Fantastic_Fest "Fantastic Fest") film festival.[[233]](#cite_note-237) In 2004 the city was first in *[MovieMaker Magazine](/wiki/MovieMaker_Magazine "MovieMaker Magazine")'s* annual top ten cities to live and make movies.[[234]](#cite_note-238)

Austin has been the location for a number of motion pictures, partly due to the influence of The [University of Texas at Austin Department of Radio-Television-Film](/wiki/University_of_Texas_at_Austin_College_of_Communication "University of Texas at Austin College of Communication"). Films produced in Austin include *[The Texas Chain Saw Massacre](/wiki/The_Texas_Chain_Saw_Massacre "The Texas Chain Saw Massacre")* (1974), *[Songwriter](/wiki/Songwriter_(film) "Songwriter (film)")* (1984), *[Man of the House](/wiki/Man_of_the_House_(2005_crime_comedy_film) "Man of the House (2005 crime comedy film)")*, *[Secondhand Lions](/wiki/Secondhand_Lions "Secondhand Lions")*, *[Texas Chainsaw Massacre 2](/wiki/Texas_Chainsaw_Massacre_2 "Texas Chainsaw Massacre 2")*, *[Nadine](/wiki/Nadine_(1987_film) "Nadine (1987 film)")*, *[Waking Life](/wiki/Waking_Life "Waking Life")*, *[Spy Kids](/wiki/Spy_Kids_(film) "Spy Kids (film)")*, *[The Faculty](/wiki/The_Faculty "The Faculty")*, [*Dazed and Confused*](/wiki/Dazed_and_Confused_(film) "Dazed and Confused (film)"), *The Guards Themselves*, *[Wild Texas Wind](/wiki/Wild_Texas_Wind "Wild Texas Wind")*, *[Office Space](/wiki/Office_Space "Office Space")*, *[The Life of David Gale](/wiki/The_Life_of_David_Gale "The Life of David Gale")*, *[Miss Congeniality](/wiki/Miss_Congeniality_(film) "Miss Congeniality (film)")*, *[Doubting Thomas](/wiki/Doubting_Thomas "Doubting Thomas")*, *[Slacker](/wiki/Slacker_(film) "Slacker (film)")*, *[Idiocracy](/wiki/Idiocracy "Idiocracy")*, *[Death Proof](/wiki/Death_Proof "Death Proof")*, *[The New Guy](/wiki/The_New_Guy "The New Guy")*, *[Hope Floats](/wiki/Hope_Floats "Hope Floats")*, *[The Alamo](/wiki/The_Alamo_(2004_film) "The Alamo (2004 film)")*, *[Blank Check](/wiki/Blank_Check_(film) "Blank Check (film)")*, *The Wendall Baker Story*, *[School of Rock](/wiki/School_of_Rock "School of Rock")*, *[A Slipping-Down Life](/wiki/A_Slipping-Down_Life "A Slipping-Down Life")*, *[A Scanner Darkly](/wiki/A_Scanner_Darkly_(film) "A Scanner Darkly (film)")*, *[Saturday Morning Massacre](/wiki/Saturday_Morning_Massacre "Saturday Morning Massacre")*, and most recently, the [Coen brothers](/wiki/Coen_brothers "Coen brothers")' *[True Grit](/wiki/True_Grit_(2010_film) "True Grit (2010 film)")*, *[Grindhouse](/wiki/Grindhouse_(film) "Grindhouse (film)")*, *[Machete](/wiki/Machete_(2010_film) "Machete (2010 film)")*, *[How to Eat Fried Worms](/wiki/How_to_Eat_Fried_Worms_(film) "How to Eat Fried Worms (film)")*, *[Bandslam](/wiki/Bandslam "Bandslam")* and *[Lazer Team](/wiki/Lazer_Team "Lazer Team")*. In order to draw future film projects to the area, the [Austin Film Society](/wiki/Austin_Film_Society "Austin Film Society") has converted several airplane hangars from the former Mueller Airport into filmmaking center [Austin Studios](/wiki/Austin_Studios "Austin Studios"). Projects that have used facilities at Austin Studios include music videos by [The Flaming Lips](/wiki/The_Flaming_Lips "The Flaming Lips") and feature films such as *[25th Hour](/wiki/25th_Hour "25th Hour")* and *[Sin City](/wiki/Sin_City_(film) "Sin City (film)")*.

Austin also hosted the [MTV](/wiki/MTV "MTV") series, *[The Real World: Austin](/wiki/The_Real_World:_Austin "The Real World: Austin")* in 2005. [Season 4](/wiki/Fear_the_Walking_Dead_(season_4) "Fear the Walking Dead (season 4)") of the [AMC](/wiki/AMC_(TV_channel) "AMC (TV channel)") show *[Fear the Walking Dead](/wiki/Fear_the_Walking_Dead "Fear the Walking Dead")* was filmed in various locations around Austin in 2018.[[235]](#cite_note-239) The film review websites [Spill.com](/wiki/Spill.com "Spill.com") and [Ain't It Cool News](/wiki/Ain%27t_It_Cool_News "Ain't It Cool News") are based in Austin. [Rooster Teeth Productions](/wiki/Rooster_Teeth_Productions "Rooster Teeth Productions"), creator of popular web series such as *[Red vs. Blue](/wiki/Red_vs._Blue "Red vs. Blue")*, *[RWBY](/wiki/RWBY "RWBY")*, and *[Camp Camp](/wiki/Camp_Camp "Camp Camp")* was also located in Austin.

### Theater

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Theater")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/State_and_Paramount_Theaters_-_Austin%2C_Texas_-_DSC08305.jpg/250px-State_and_Paramount_Theaters_-_Austin%2C_Texas_-_DSC08305.jpg)](/wiki/File:State_and_Paramount_Theaters_-_Austin,_Texas_-_DSC08305.jpg)

The State Theater and [Paramount Theatre](/wiki/Paramount_Theatre_(Austin,_Texas) "Paramount Theatre (Austin, Texas)") on Congress Avenue in Downtown Austin

Austin has a strong theater culture, with dozens of itinerant and resident companies producing a variety of work. A volunteer-run arts organization supporting creative expression and counter-culture community - Church of the Friendly Ghost (COTFG) helped many experimental programs get their start in Austin, TX.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The city also has live performance theater venues such as the [Zachary Scott Theatre Center](/wiki/Zachary_Scott_Theatre_Center "Zachary Scott Theatre Center"), Vortex Repertory Company, Salvage Vanguard Theater, Rude Mechanicals' the Off Center, Austin Playhouse, Scottish Rite Children's Theater, [Hyde Park Theatre](/wiki/Hyde_Park_Theatre "Hyde Park Theatre"), the Blue Theater, The Hideout Theatre, and [Esther's Follies](/wiki/Esther%27s_Follies "Esther's Follies").[[236]](#cite_note-240) The [Victory Grill](/wiki/Victory_Grill "Victory Grill") was a renowned venue on the [Chitlin' Circuit](/wiki/Chitlin%27_Circuit "Chitlin' Circuit").[[237]](#cite_note-241) Public art and performances in the parks and on bridges are popular. Austin hosts the Fuse Box Festival each April featuring theater artists.[[238]](#cite_note-242)

The [Paramount Theatre](/wiki/Paramount_Theatre_(Austin,_Texas) "Paramount Theatre (Austin, Texas)"), opened in downtown Austin in 1915, contributes to Austin's theater and film culture, showing classic films throughout the summer and hosting regional premieres for films such as *[Miss Congeniality](/wiki/Miss_Congeniality_(film) "Miss Congeniality (film)")*.[[239]](#cite_note-243) The [Zilker Park](/wiki/Zilker_Park "Zilker Park") Summer Musical is a long-running outdoor musical.[[240]](#cite_note-244)

The [Long Center for the Performing Arts](/wiki/Long_Center_for_the_Performing_Arts "Long Center for the Performing Arts") is a 2,300-seat theater built partly with materials reused from the old Lester E. Palmer Auditorium.

[Ballet Austin](/wiki/Ballet_Austin "Ballet Austin") is among the fifteen largest ballet academies in the country.[[241]](#cite_note-245) Each year Ballet Austin's 20-member professional company performs ballets from a wide variety of choreographers, including their artistic director, [Stephen Mills](/wiki/Stephen_Mills "Stephen Mills"). The city is also home to the [Ballet East Dance Company](/wiki/Ballet_East_Dance_Company "Ballet East Dance Company"), a modern dance ensemble, and the [Tapestry Dance Company](/wiki/Tapestry_Dance_Company "Tapestry Dance Company") which performs a variety of dance genres.

The Austin [improvisational theatre](/wiki/Improvisational_theatre "Improvisational theatre") scene has several theaters: ColdTowne Theater, The Hideout Theater, and The Fallout Theater. Austin also hosts the Out of Bounds Comedy Festival, which draws comedic artists in all disciplines to Austin.

### Libraries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Libraries")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Austin_public_library_opened_October_28_2017.jpg/250px-Austin_public_library_opened_October_28_2017.jpg)](/wiki/File:Austin_public_library_opened_October_28_2017.jpg)

View of Austin Central Library from César Chávez Street

The [Austin Public Library](/wiki/Austin_Public_Library "Austin Public Library") is operated by the City of Austin and consists of the Central Library on [César Chávez Street](/wiki/Cesar_Chavez_Street_(Austin) "Cesar Chavez Street (Austin)"), the [Austin History Center](/wiki/Austin_History_Center "Austin History Center"), 20 branches and the Recycled Reads bookstore and [upcycling](/wiki/Upcycling "Upcycling") facility.[[242]](#cite_note-246) The APL library system also has mobile libraries – bookmobile buses and a human-powered trike and trailer called "unbound: sin fronteras."[[243]](#cite_note-247)[[244]](#cite_note-248)

The Central Library, which is an anchor to the redevelopment of the former [Seaholm Power Plant](/wiki/Seaholm_Power_Plant "Seaholm Power Plant") site and the [Shoal Creek](/wiki/Shoal_Creek,_Austin,_Texas "Shoal Creek, Austin, Texas") Walk, opened on October 28, 2017.[[245]](#cite_note-249) The six-story Central Library contains a living [rooftop garden](/wiki/Rooftop_garden "Rooftop garden"), reading porches, an indoor reading room, [bicycle parking station](/wiki/Bicycle_parking_station "Bicycle parking station"), large indoor and outdoor event spaces, a gift shop, an art gallery, café, and a "technology petting zoo" where visitors can play with next-generation gadgets like [3D printers](/wiki/3D_printing "3D printing").[[246]](#cite_note-250) In 2018, [Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)") magazine named the Austin Central Library on its list of "World's Greatest Places."[[247]](#cite_note-251)

### Museums and other points of interest

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Museums and other points of interest")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/LBJ_Library_2017.jpg/250px-LBJ_Library_2017.jpg)](/wiki/File:LBJ_Library_2017.jpg)

The [Lyndon Baines Johnson Presidential Library](/wiki/Lyndon_Baines_Johnson_Presidential_Library "Lyndon Baines Johnson Presidential Library") on the [University of Texas](/wiki/University_of_Texas "University of Texas") campus in Austin

Museums in Austin include the [Texas Science and Natural History Museum](/wiki/Texas_Science_and_Natural_History_Museum "Texas Science and Natural History Museum"), the [George Washington Carver Museum and Cultural Center](/wiki/George_Washington_Carver_Museum_and_Cultural_Center "George Washington Carver Museum and Cultural Center"), [Thinkery](/wiki/Thinkery "Thinkery"), the [Blanton Museum of Art](/wiki/Blanton_Museum_of_Art "Blanton Museum of Art") (reopened in 2006), the [Bob Bullock Texas State History Museum](/wiki/Bob_Bullock_Texas_State_History_Museum "Bob Bullock Texas State History Museum") across the street (which opened in 2000), [The Contemporary Austin](/wiki/The_Contemporary_Austin "The Contemporary Austin"), the [Elisabet Ney Museum](/wiki/Elisabet_Ney_Museum "Elisabet Ney Museum"), the [Women and Their Work](/wiki/Women_and_Their_Work "Women and Their Work") gallery, and the galleries at the [Harry Ransom Center](/wiki/Harry_Ransom_Center "Harry Ransom Center"). The [Texas State Capitol](/wiki/Texas_State_Capitol "Texas State Capitol") itself is also a major tourist attraction.

The [Driskill Hotel](/wiki/Driskill_Hotel "Driskill Hotel"), built in 1886, once owned by [George W. Littlefield](/wiki/George_W._Littlefield "George W. Littlefield"), and located at 6th and Brazos streets, was finished just before the construction of the Capitol building. [Sixth Street](/wiki/6th_Street_(Austin) "6th Street (Austin)") is a musical hub for the city. The Enchanted Forest, a multi-acre outdoor music, art, and performance art space in South Austin hosts events such as fire-dancing and circus-like-acts.[[248]](#cite_note-252) Austin is also home to the [Lyndon Baines Johnson Library and Museum](/wiki/Lyndon_Baines_Johnson_Library_and_Museum "Lyndon Baines Johnson Library and Museum"), which houses documents and artifacts related to the Johnson administration, including LBJ's limousine and a re-creation of the [Oval Office](/wiki/Oval_Office "Oval Office").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/TMI_castle_austin_2014.jpg/250px-TMI_castle_austin_2014.jpg)](/wiki/File:TMI_castle_austin_2014.jpg)

The [HOPE Outdoor Gallery](/wiki/HOPE_Outdoor_Gallery "HOPE Outdoor Gallery"), overlooked by the historic Texas Military Academy building, the oldest standing educational building in Texas; the gallery has since been demolished.

Locally produced art is featured at the [South Austin Museum of Popular Culture](/wiki/South_Austin_Museum_of_Popular_Culture "South Austin Museum of Popular Culture"). The [Mexic-Arte Museum](/wiki/Mexic-Arte_Museum "Mexic-Arte Museum") is a Mexican and Mexican-American art museum founded in 1983. Austin is also home to the O. Henry House Museum, which served as the residence of [O. Henry](/wiki/O._Henry "O. Henry") from 1893 to 1895. Farmers' markets are popular attractions, providing a variety of locally grown and often organic foods.[[249]](#cite_note-253)

Austin also has many odd statues and landmarks, such as the *[Stevie Ray Vaughan Memorial](/wiki/Stevie_Ray_Vaughan_Memorial "Stevie Ray Vaughan Memorial")*, the [*Willie Nelson* statue](/wiki/Willie_Nelson_statue "Willie Nelson statue"), the Mangia dinosaur, the Loca Maria lady at Taco Xpress, the Hyde Park Gym's giant flexed arm, and [Daniel Johnston](/wiki/Daniel_Johnston "Daniel Johnston")'s *[Hi, How are You](/wiki/Hi,_How_are_You "Hi, How are You")?* Jeremiah the Innocent frog mural.[[250]](#cite_note-254)

The [Ann W. Richards Congress Avenue Bridge](/wiki/Ann_W._Richards_Congress_Avenue_Bridge "Ann W. Richards Congress Avenue Bridge") houses the world's largest urban population of [Mexican free-tailed bats](/wiki/Mexican_free-tailed_bat "Mexican free-tailed bat"). Starting in March,[[251]](#cite_note-255)[[252]](#cite_note-256) up to 1.5 million bats take up residence inside the bridge's expansion and contraction zones as well as in long horizontal grooves running the length of the bridge's underside, an environment ideally suited for raising their young. Every evening around sunset, the bats emerge in search of insects, an exit visible on [weather radar](/wiki/Weather_radar "Weather radar"). Watching the bat emergence is an event that is popular with locals and tourists, with more than 100,000 viewers per year. The bats migrate to Mexico each winter.[[253]](#cite_note-257)

The [Austin Zoo](/wiki/Austin_Zoo "Austin Zoo"), located in [unincorporated](/wiki/Unincorporated_area "Unincorporated area") western [Travis County](/wiki/Travis_County,_Texas "Travis County, Texas"), is a [rescue zoo](/wiki/Rescue_coordination_centre "Rescue coordination centre") that provides sanctuary to displaced animals from a variety of situations, including those involving neglect.

The HOPE Outdoor Gallery was a public, three-story outdoor [street art](/wiki/Street_art "Street art") project located on Baylor Street in the [Clarksville](/wiki/Clarksville,_Austin,_Texas "Clarksville, Austin, Texas") neighborhood.[[254]](#cite_note-258) The gallery, which consisted of the foundations of a failed multifamily development,[[255]](#cite_note-259) was a constantly-evolving canvas of [graffiti](/wiki/Graffiti "Graffiti") and [murals](/wiki/Murals "Murals"). Also known as "Castle Hill" or simply "Graffiti Park", the site on Baylor Street was closed to the public in early January 2019 but remained intact, behind a fence and with an armed guard, in mid-March 2019.[[256]](#cite_note-260) The gallery will build a new art park at Carson Creek Ranch in Southeast Austin.[[257]](#cite_note-261)

[Q2 Stadium](/wiki/Q2_Stadium "Q2 Stadium") of [Austin FC](/wiki/Austin_FC "Austin FC")

Many Austinites support the athletic programs of the University of Texas at Austin known as the [Texas Longhorns](/wiki/Texas_Longhorns "Texas Longhorns"). During the 2005–2006 academic term, the [Longhorns football team](/wiki/Texas_Longhorns_football "Texas Longhorns football") was named the [NCAA Division I FBS National Football Champion](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_FBS_National_Football_Championship "NCAA Division I FBS National Football Championship"), and the [Longhorns baseball team](/wiki/Texas_Longhorns_baseball "Texas Longhorns baseball") won the [College World Series](/wiki/2005_College_World_Series "2005 College World Series").[[258]](#cite_note-262)[[259]](#cite_note-263) The football team plays its home games in the state's second-largest sports stadium, [Darrell K Royal–Texas Memorial Stadium](/wiki/Darrell_K_Royal%E2%80%93Texas_Memorial_Stadium "Darrell K Royal–Texas Memorial Stadium"), seating over 101,000 fans.[[260]](#cite_note-264) Baseball games are played at [UFCU Disch–Falk Field](/wiki/UFCU_Disch%E2%80%93Falk_Field "UFCU Disch–Falk Field").

Austin was the most populous city in the United States without a [major-league professional sports team](/wiki/Major_professional_sports_leagues_of_the_United_States_and_Canada "Major professional sports leagues of the United States and Canada"), which changed in 2021 with [Austin FC](/wiki/Austin_FC "Austin FC")'s entry to MLS.[[261]](#cite_note-265) Minor-league professional sports came to Austin in 1996, when the [Austin Ice Bats](/wiki/Austin_Ice_Bats "Austin Ice Bats") began playing at the Travis County Expo Center;[[262]](#cite_note-266) they were later replaced by the [AHL](/wiki/American_Hockey_League "American Hockey League") [Texas Stars](/wiki/Texas_Stars "Texas Stars").[[263]](#cite_note-267) Austin has hosted a number of other professional teams, including the [Austin Spurs](/wiki/Austin_Spurs "Austin Spurs") of the [NBA G League](/wiki/NBA_G_League "NBA G League"),[[264]](#cite_note-268) the [Austin Aztex](/wiki/Austin_Aztex "Austin Aztex") of the [United Soccer League](/wiki/United_Soccer_League "United Soccer League"), the [Austin Outlaws](/wiki/Austin_Outlaws "Austin Outlaws") in [WFA](/wiki/Women%27s_Football_Alliance "Women's Football Alliance") football, and the [Austin Aces](/wiki/Austin_Aces "Austin Aces") in [WTT](/wiki/World_TeamTennis "World TeamTennis") tennis.

Natural features like the bicycle-friendly [Texas Hill Country](/wiki/Texas_Hill_Country "Texas Hill Country") and generally mild [climate](#Climate) make Austin the home of several endurance and multi-sport races and communities. The Capitol 10,000 is the largest 10 k race in Texas, and approximately fifth largest in the United States.[[265]](#cite_note-269) The [Austin Marathon](/wiki/Austin_Marathon "Austin Marathon") has been run in the city every year since 1992. Additionally, the city is home to the largest 5 mile race in Texas,[[266]](#cite_note-270) named the Turkey Trot as it is run annually on Thanksgiving. Started in 1991 by Thundercloud Subs, a local sandwich chain (who still sponsors the event), the event has grown to host over 20,000 runners. All proceeds are donated to Caritas of Austin, a local charity.

The Austin-founded American Swimming Association hosts several swim races around town. Austin is also the hometown of several cycling groups and the disgraced cyclist [Lance Armstrong](/wiki/Lance_Armstrong "Lance Armstrong").[[267]](#cite_note-271) Combining these three disciplines is a growing crop of triathlons, including the Capital of Texas Triathlon held every [Memorial Day](/wiki/Memorial_Day "Memorial Day") on and around Lady Bird Lake, [Auditorium Shores](/wiki/Auditorium_Shores "Auditorium Shores"), and [Downtown Austin](/wiki/Downtown_Austin "Downtown Austin").[[268]](#cite_note-272)

Austin is home to the [Circuit of the Americas](/wiki/Circuit_of_the_Americas "Circuit of the Americas") (COTA), a grade 1 [Fédération Internationale de l'Automobile](/wiki/F%C3%A9d%C3%A9ration_Internationale_de_l%27Automobile "Fédération Internationale de l'Automobile") specification 3.427-mile (5.515 km) [motor racing](/wiki/Motor_racing "Motor racing") facility which hosts the [Formula One](/wiki/Formula_One "Formula One") [United States Grand Prix](/wiki/United_States_Grand_Prix "United States Grand Prix").[[269]](#cite_note-273) The State of Texas has pledged $25 million in public funds annually for 10 years to pay the sanctioning fees for the race.[[270]](#cite_note-274)[[271]](#cite_note-275) Built at an estimated cost of $250 to $300 million, the circuit opened in 2012 and is located just east of the [Austin Bergstrom International Airport](/wiki/Austin_Bergstrom_International_Airport "Austin Bergstrom International Airport").[[272]](#cite_note-276)[[273]](#cite_note-277) The circuit also hosts the [EchoPark Automotive Grand Prix](/wiki/Texas_Grand_Prix "Texas Grand Prix") NASCAR race in late March each year.

The summer of 2014 marked the inaugural season for [World TeamTennis](/wiki/World_TeamTennis "World TeamTennis") team [Austin Aces](/wiki/Austin_Aces "Austin Aces"), formerly [Orange County Breakers](/wiki/Orange_County_Breakers "Orange County Breakers") of the southern California region. The Austin Aces played their matches at the [Cedar Park Center](/wiki/Cedar_Park_Center "Cedar Park Center") northwest of Austin, and featured former professionals [Andy Roddick](/wiki/Andy_Roddick "Andy Roddick") and [Marion Bartoli](/wiki/Marion_Bartoli "Marion Bartoli"), as well as current WTA tour player [Vera Zvonareva](/wiki/Vera_Zvonareva "Vera Zvonareva").[[274]](#cite_note-278) The team left after the 2015 season.

In 2017, [Precourt Sports Ventures](/wiki/Anthony_Precourt "Anthony Precourt") announced a plan to move the [Columbus Crew SC](/wiki/Columbus_Crew_SC "Columbus Crew SC") soccer franchise from [Columbus, Ohio](/wiki/Columbus,_Ohio "Columbus, Ohio") to Austin.[[275]](#cite_note-279) Precourt negotiated an agreement with the City of Austin to build a $200 million privately funded stadium on public land at 10414 McKalla Place,[[276]](#cite_note-280) following initial interest in Butler Shores Metropolitan Park and Roy G. Guerrero Colorado River Park.[[277]](#cite_note-281) As part of an arrangement with the league, operational rights of Columbus Crew SC were sold in late 2018,[[278]](#cite_note-282) and Austin FC was announced as Major League Soccer's 27th franchise on January 15, 2019, with the expansion team starting play in 2021.[[279]](#cite_note-283)

The [Austin Country Club](/wiki/Austin_Country_Club "Austin Country Club") is a private golf club located along the shores of the Colorado River, right next to the [Pennybacker Bridge](/wiki/Pennybacker_Bridge "Pennybacker Bridge"). Founded in 1899, the club moved to its third and present site in 1984, which features a challenging layout designed by noted course architect [Pete Dye](/wiki/Pete_Dye "Pete Dye").[[280]](#cite_note-284)[[281]](#cite_note-285)

Austin is set to host the [BLAST.TV Austin Major](/wiki/Blast_Austin_Major_2025 "Blast Austin Major 2025"), the 22nd [*Counter-Strike* Major](/wiki/Counter-Strike_Major_Championships "Counter-Strike Major Championships") [esports](/wiki/Esports "Esports") tournament, from June 9 to 22, 2025.[[282]](#cite_note-286)

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Government")]

### City government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: City government")]

See also: [List of mayors of Austin, Texas](/wiki/List_of_mayors_of_Austin,_Texas "List of mayors of Austin, Texas") and [Austin City Council](/wiki/Austin_City_Council "Austin City Council")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/AustinTXCityHall.JPG/250px-AustinTXCityHall.JPG)](/wiki/File:AustinTXCityHall.JPG)

Austin City Hall

Austin is administered by an 11-member [city council](/wiki/City_council "City council") (10 council members elected by geographic district plus a mayor elected at large). The council is accompanied by a hired [city manager](/wiki/City_manager "City manager") under the [manager-council system of municipal governance](/wiki/Council-manager_government "Council-manager government"). Council and mayoral elections are non-partisan, with a runoff in case there is no majority winner. A referendum approved by voters on November 6, 2012, changed the council composition from six council members plus a mayor elected at large to the current "10+1" district system. Supporters maintained that the at-large system would increase participation for all areas of the city, especially for those which had lacked representation from City Council.[[283]](#cite_note-287)

November 2014 marked the first election under the new system. The [Federal government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States") had forced San Antonio and Dallas to abandon at-large systems before 1987; however, the court could not show a racist pattern in Austin and upheld the city's at-large system during a 1984 lawsuit. In five elections between 1973 and 1994 Austin voters rejected single-member districts.[[284]](#cite_note-288)

Austin formerly operated its city hall at 128 West 8th Street.[[285]](#cite_note-289) Antoine Predock and Cotera Kolar Negrete & Reed Architects designed a new city hall building, which was intended to reflect what *[The Dallas Morning News](/wiki/The_Dallas_Morning_News "The Dallas Morning News")* referred to as a "crazy-quilt vitality, that embraces everything from country music to environmental protests and high-tech swagger."[[286]](#cite_note-290) The new city hall, built from recycled materials, has solar panels in its garage.[[287]](#cite_note-291) The city hall, at 301 West Second Street, opened in November 2004.[[288]](#cite_note-292) [Kirk Watson](/wiki/Kirk_Watson "Kirk Watson") is the current mayor of Austin, assuming the office for a second non-consecutive term on January 6, 2023.

In the 2012 elections, City Council elections were moved from May to November and City council members were given staggered term limits[[289]](#cite_note-293) In 2022 Proposition D moved the term of the Austin Mayor to coincide with Presidential election years, so [Kirk Watson](/wiki/Kirk_Watson "Kirk Watson") would only serve two years unlike his predecessor [Steve Adler](/wiki/Steve_Adler_(politician) "Steve Adler (politician)")[[290]](#cite_note-294)

Law enforcement in Austin is provided by the [Austin Police Department](/wiki/Austin_Police_Department "Austin Police Department"), except for state government buildings, which are patrolled by the [Texas Department of Public Safety](/wiki/Texas_Department_of_Public_Safety "Texas Department of Public Safety"). The University of Texas Police operate from the [University of Texas](/wiki/University_of_Texas_at_Austin "University of Texas at Austin").

Fire protection within the city limits is provided by the [Austin Fire Department](/wiki/Austin_Fire_Department "Austin Fire Department"), while the surrounding county is divided into twelve geographical areas known as emergency services districts, which are covered by separate regional fire departments.[[291]](#cite_note-295) Emergency medical services are provided for the whole county by Austin-Travis County Emergency Medical Services.[[292]](#cite_note-296)[[293]](#cite_note-297)

In 2003, the city adopted a resolution against the [USA PATRIOT Act](/wiki/USA_PATRIOT_Act "USA PATRIOT Act") that reaffirmed constitutionally guaranteed rights.

The current mayor of Austin is [Kirk Watson](/wiki/Kirk_Watson "Kirk Watson").[[294]](#cite_note-298) As of 2025, though positions are officiallly nonpartisan, all elected member of the city council are [Democrats](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)").[[295]](#cite_note-299)

### Crime

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Crime")]

As of 2019, Austin is one of the safest large cities in the United States.[[302]](#cite_note-306) In 2019, the FBI named Austin the 11th safest city on a list of 22 American cities with a population above 400,000.[[303]](#cite_note-307)

FBI statistics show that overall violent and property crimes dropped in Austin in 2015, but increased in suburban areas of the city.[[304]](#cite_note-308) One such southeastern suburb, [Del Valle](/wiki/Del_Valle,_Texas "Del Valle, Texas"), reported eight homicides within two months in 2016.[[305]](#cite_note-309) According to 2016 [APD](/wiki/Austin_Police_Department "Austin Police Department") crime statistics, the 78723 census tract had the most violent crime, with 6 murders, 25 rapes, and 81 robberies.[[306]](#cite_note-310) The city had 39 homicides in 2016, the most since 1997.[[307]](#cite_note-311)[[308]](#cite_note-312)

#### Notable incidents

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Notable incidents")]

In 1884 and 1885, one of the earliest recorded [serial killings](/wiki/Serial_killer "Serial killer") in the United States occurred in Austin, in which 8 people were murdered by a suspect known as the "[Servant Girl Annihilator](/wiki/Servant_Girl_Annihilator "Servant Girl Annihilator")".[[309]](#cite_note-TexasMonthly-313)[[310]](#cite_note-314)[[311]](#cite_note-315)

One of the first American [mass](/wiki/Mass_shootings_in_the_United_States "Mass shootings in the United States") [school shooting](/wiki/School_shooting "School shooting") incidents took place in Austin on August 1, 1966, when [Charles Whitman](/wiki/Charles_Whitman "Charles Whitman") shot 43 people, killing 13 from the top of the University of Texas tower.[[312]](#cite_note-texasmonthly.com-316) The [University of Texas tower shooting](/wiki/University_of_Texas_tower_shooting "University of Texas tower shooting") led to the formation of the [SWAT](/wiki/SWAT "SWAT") team of the [Austin Police Department](/wiki/Austin_Police_Department "Austin Police Department").[[312]](#cite_note-texasmonthly.com-316)[[313]](#cite_note-317)[[314]](#cite_note-318)

In 1991, [four teenage girls were murdered in a yogurt shop](/wiki/1991_Austin_yogurt_shop_murders "1991 Austin yogurt shop murders") by serial killer [Robert Eugene Brashers](/wiki/Robert_Eugene_Brashers "Robert Eugene Brashers").[[315]](#cite_note-cjn3o35-319) A police officer responded to reports of a fire at the [I Can't Believe It's Yogurt!](/wiki/I_Can%27t_Believe_It%27s_Yogurt! "I Can't Believe It's Yogurt!") store on Anderson Lane and discovered the girls' bodies in a back room. The murders were unsolved until 2025.[[316]](#cite_note-320)

In 2010, Andrew Joseph Stack III deliberately crashed his [Piper PA-28 Cherokee](/wiki/Piper_PA-28_Cherokee "Piper PA-28 Cherokee") into Echelon 1, a building in which the [Internal Revenue Service](/wiki/Internal_Revenue_Service "Internal Revenue Service") was a lessee of, housing 190 employees.[[317]](#cite_note-321) The resulting explosion killed one and injured 13 IRS employees, partially damaged the building and cost the IRS a total of $38.6 million.[[318]](#cite_note-322) *(see [2010 Austin suicide attack](/wiki/2010_Austin_suicide_attack "2010 Austin suicide attack"))*

A [series of bombings](/wiki/Austin_serial_bombings "Austin serial bombings") occurred in Austin in March 2018. Over the course of 20 days, five [package bombs](/wiki/Letter_bomb "Letter bomb") exploded, killing two people and injuring another five. The suspect, 23-year-old Mark Anthony Conditt of [Pflugerville, Texas](/wiki/Pflugerville,_Texas "Pflugerville, Texas"), [blew himself up](/wiki/Suicide_bombing "Suicide bombing") inside his vehicle after he was pulled over by police on March 21, also injuring a police officer.[[319]](#cite_note-time_Aust-323)[[320]](#cite_note-npr._Mark-324)

In 2020, Austin was the victim of a [cyberattack](/wiki/Cyberattack "Cyberattack") by the Russian group [Berserk Bear](/wiki/Berserk_Bear "Berserk Bear"), possibly related to the [U.S. federal government data breach](/wiki/2020_United_States_federal_government_data_breach "2020 United States federal government data breach") earlier that year.[[321]](#cite_note-intercept-2020-12-17-325)[[322]](#cite_note-326)

On April 18, 2021, a [shooting](/wiki/Shooting "Shooting") occurred at the Arboretum Oaks Apartments near [The Arboretum](/wiki/The_Arboretum_(Austin,_Texas) "The Arboretum (Austin, Texas)") [shopping center](/wiki/Shopping_center "Shopping center"), in which a former [Travis County](/wiki/Travis_County "Travis County") Sheriff's Office [detective](/wiki/Detective "Detective") killed his ex-wife, his adoptive daughter, and his daughter's boyfriend.[[323]](#cite_note-327)[[324]](#cite_note-Statesman.shooting-328)[[325]](#cite_note-329) The suspect, who was previously charged with [child sexual assault](/wiki/Child_sexual_assault "Child sexual assault"), was arrested in [Manor](/wiki/Manor,_Texas "Manor, Texas") after a 20-hour manhunt.[[326]](#cite_note-CNN.Shooting-330)[[327]](#cite_note-USATODAY.Shooting-331)

A mass shooting took place in the early morning of June 12, 2021, on [Sixth Street](/wiki/Sixth_Street_(Austin,_Texas) "Sixth Street (Austin, Texas)"), which resulted in 14 people injured and one dead. The man killed was believed to be an innocent bystander who was struck as he was standing outside a bar. A 19-year-old suspect, De'ondre "Dre" White, was formally charged and arrested in [Killeen](/wiki/Killeen,_Texas "Killeen, Texas") nearly two weeks after the shooting.[[328]](#cite_note-332)[[329]](#cite_note-333)[[330]](#cite_note-334)

In 2024, Zacharia Doar, a 23-year old Palestinian-American man, was attacked and stabbed in the chest on West 26th Street, [West Campus](/wiki/West_Campus,_Austin,_Texas "West Campus, Austin, Texas"), after returning from a rally in support of Palestinian human rights. The assailant, 36-year old Bert James Baker was arrested at the scene and charged with aggravated assault with a deadly weapon.[[331]](#cite_note-335)[[332]](#cite_note-336)

On August 11, 2025, a [mass shooting](/wiki/Mass_shooting "Mass shooting") occurred outside a [Target](/wiki/Target_Corporation "Target Corporation") store in North Austin, killing three people. The suspect fled the Target by [hijacking](/wiki/Carjacking "Carjacking") a [car](/wiki/Car "Car") belonging to one of the victims. After leaving the Target area, the suspect crashed the stolen [vehicle](/wiki/Vehicle "Vehicle") and proceeded to steal another vehicle from a [car dealership](/wiki/Car_dealership "Car dealership"). The suspect was later taken into custody after being subdued with a [Taser](/wiki/Taser "Taser"). He was later identified as a 32-year-old male.[[333]](#cite_note-337)[[334]](#cite_note-338)[[335]](#cite_note-339)

### Other levels of government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Other levels of government")]

See also: [Government of Texas](/wiki/Government_of_Texas "Government of Texas") and [List of capitals in the United States](/wiki/List_of_capitals_in_the_United_States "List of capitals in the United States")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Federal_Courthouse%2C_Austin%2C_TX_IMG_6339.JPG/250px-Federal_Courthouse%2C_Austin%2C_TX_IMG_6339.JPG)](/wiki/File:Federal_Courthouse,_Austin,_TX_IMG_6339.JPG)

The 8-story U.S. Courthouse is located at Fourth, Fifth, San Antonio, and Nueces streets (opened December 2012).[[336]](#cite_note-Castillo_2012-340)

Austin is the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") of [Travis County](/wiki/Travis_County,_Texas "Travis County, Texas") and hosts the [Heman Marion Sweatt Travis County Courthouse](/wiki/Heman_Marion_Sweatt_Travis_County_Courthouse "Heman Marion Sweatt Travis County Courthouse") downtown, as well as other county government offices. The [Texas Department of Transportation](/wiki/Texas_Department_of_Transportation "Texas Department of Transportation") operates the Austin District Office in Austin.[[337]](#cite_note-341) The [Texas Department of Criminal Justice](/wiki/Texas_Department_of_Criminal_Justice "Texas Department of Criminal Justice") (TDCJ) operates the Austin I and Austin II district parole offices in Austin.[[338]](#cite_note-342) The [United States Postal Service](/wiki/United_States_Postal_Service "United States Postal Service") operates several post offices in Austin.

### Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Politics")]

Former Governor [Rick Perry](/wiki/Rick_Perry "Rick Perry") had previously referred to it as a "blueberry in the tomato soup", meaning, Austin had previously been a Democratic city in a Republican state.[[339]](#cite_note-343) However, Texas currently has multiple urban cities also voting Democratic and electing Democratic mayors in elections.[[340]](#cite_note-344)

After the most recent redistricting, Austin is currently divided between the [10th](/wiki/Texas%27s_10th_congressional_district "Texas's 10th congressional district"), [35th](/wiki/Texas%27s_35th_congressional_district "Texas's 35th congressional district") and [37th Congressional districts](/wiki/Texas%27s_37th_congressional_district "Texas's 37th congressional district").[[341]](#cite_note-345)
