#### Issues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Issues")]

A controversial turning point in the political history of the Austin area was the [2003 Texas redistricting](/wiki/2003_Texas_redistricting "2003 Texas redistricting"). Before then, Austin had been entirely or almost entirely within the borders of a single congressional district–what was then the 10th District–for over a century. Opponents characterized the resulting district layout as excessively partisan [gerrymandering](/wiki/Gerrymandering "Gerrymandering"), and the plan was challenged in court by Democratic and minority activists. The [Supreme Court of the United States](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States") has never struck down a redistricting plan for being excessively partisan. The plan was subsequently upheld by a three-judge federal panel in late 2003, and on June 28, 2006, the matter was largely settled when the Supreme Court, in a 7–2 decision, upheld the entire congressional redistricting plan with the exception of a Hispanic-majority district in southwest Texas. This affected Austin's districting, as U.S. Rep. [Lloyd Doggett](/wiki/Lloyd_Doggett "Lloyd Doggett")'s district (U.S. Congressional District 25) was found to be insufficiently compact to compensate for the reduced [minority influence](/wiki/Minority_influence "Minority influence") in the southwest district; it was redrawn so that it took in most of southeastern Travis County and several counties to its south and east.[[342]](#cite_note-346)

#### Environmental movement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Environmental movement")]

The distinguishing political movement of Austin politics has been that of the [environmental movement](/wiki/Environmental_movement "Environmental movement"), which spawned the parallel neighborhood movement, then the more recent conservationist movement (as typified by the Hill Country Conservancy),[[343]](#cite_note-347) and eventually the current ongoing debate about "sense of place" and preserving the Austin quality of life. Much of the environmental movement has matured into a debate on issues related to saving and creating an Austin "sense of place."[[193]](#cite_note-Swearingen_2010-197) In 2012, Austin became just one of a few cities in [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas") to ban the sale and use of plastic bags. However, the ban ended in 2018 due to a court ruling that regarded all bag bans in the state to contravene the Texas Solid Waste Disposal Act.[[344]](#cite_note-Hoffberger-348) In 2016, Austin became the first Gold designee of the [SolSmart](/wiki/SolSmart "SolSmart") program,[[345]](#cite_note-349) a national program from the [U.S. Department of Energy](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Energy "United States Department of Energy") that recognizes local governments for enacting solar-friendly measures at the local level.[[346]](#cite_note-350)[[347]](#cite_note-351)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Education")]

According to the 2015–2019 Census estimates, 51.7% of Austin residents aged 25 and over have earned at least a [bachelor's degree](/wiki/Bachelor%27s_degree "Bachelor's degree"), compared with the national figure of 32.1%. 19.4% hold a [graduate](/wiki/Graduate_degree "Graduate degree") or [professional degree](/wiki/Professional_degree "Professional degree"), compared with the national figure of 12.4%.[[348]](#cite_note-352)

### Higher education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Higher education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Texas_University_at_Austin_%288095546843%29.jpg/250px-Texas_University_at_Austin_%288095546843%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Texas_University_at_Austin_(8095546843).jpg)

[The University of Texas at Austin](/wiki/University_of_Texas_at_Austin "University of Texas at Austin")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Austin_Community_College_campus_in_2013.jpg/250px-Austin_Community_College_campus_in_2013.jpg)](/wiki/File:Austin_Community_College_campus_in_2013.jpg)

[Austin Community College](/wiki/Austin_Community_College_District "Austin Community College District")

Austin is home to the [University of Texas at Austin](/wiki/University_of_Texas_at_Austin "University of Texas at Austin"), the [flagship institution](/wiki/Flagship_institution "Flagship institution") of the [University of Texas System](/wiki/University_of_Texas_System "University of Texas System") with over 40,000 undergraduate students and 11,000 graduate students.[[349]](#cite_note-353)

Other institutions of higher learning in Austin include [St. Edward's University](/wiki/St._Edward%27s_University "St. Edward's University"), [Huston–Tillotson University](/wiki/Huston%E2%80%93Tillotson_University "Huston–Tillotson University"), [Austin Community College](/wiki/Austin_Community_College "Austin Community College"), [Concordia University](/wiki/Concordia_University_Texas "Concordia University Texas"), the [Seminary of the Southwest](/wiki/Seminary_of_the_Southwest "Seminary of the Southwest"), [Texas Health and Science University](/wiki/Texas_Health_and_Science_University "Texas Health and Science University"), [University of St. Augustine for Health Sciences](/wiki/University_of_St._Augustine_for_Health_Sciences "University of St. Augustine for Health Sciences"), [Austin Graduate School of Theology](/wiki/Austin_Graduate_School_of_Theology "Austin Graduate School of Theology"), [Austin Presbyterian Theological Seminary](/wiki/Austin_Presbyterian_Theological_Seminary "Austin Presbyterian Theological Seminary"), [Virginia College](/wiki/Virginia_College "Virginia College")'s Austin Campus, [The Art Institute of Austin](/wiki/The_Art_Institute_of_Austin "The Art Institute of Austin"), Southern Careers Institute of Austin, Austin Conservatory and branch campuses of [Case Western Reserve University](/wiki/Case_Western_Reserve_University "Case Western Reserve University") and [Park University](/wiki/Park_University "Park University").

The [University of Texas System](/wiki/University_of_Texas_System "University of Texas System") and [Texas State University System](/wiki/Texas_State_University_System "Texas State University System") are headquartered in downtown Austin.

### Public primary and secondary education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: Public primary and secondary education")]

Approximately half of the city by area is served by the [Austin Independent School District](/wiki/Austin_Independent_School_District "Austin Independent School District"). This district includes notable schools such as the magnet [Liberal Arts and Science Academy High School of Austin, Texas](/wiki/Liberal_Arts_and_Science_Academy_High_School_of_Austin,_Texas "Liberal Arts and Science Academy High School of Austin, Texas") (LASA), which, by test scores, has consistently been within the top thirty high schools in the nation, as well as [The Ann Richards School for Young Women Leaders](/wiki/The_Ann_Richards_School_for_Young_Women_Leaders "The Ann Richards School for Young Women Leaders"). The remaining portion of Austin is served by adjoining school districts, including [Round Rock ISD](/wiki/Round_Rock_Independent_School_District "Round Rock Independent School District"), [Pflugerville ISD](/wiki/Pflugerville_Independent_School_District "Pflugerville Independent School District"), [Leander ISD](/wiki/Leander_Independent_School_District "Leander Independent School District"), [Manor ISD](/wiki/Manor_Independent_School_District "Manor Independent School District"), [Del Valle ISD](/wiki/Del_Valle_Independent_School_District "Del Valle Independent School District"), [Lake Travis ISD](/wiki/Lake_Travis_Independent_School_District "Lake Travis Independent School District"), [Hays ISD](/wiki/Hays_Consolidated_Independent_School_District "Hays Consolidated Independent School District"), and [Eanes](/wiki/Eanes_Independent_School_District "Eanes Independent School District") [ISD](/wiki/Independent_school_district "Independent school district").[[350]](#cite_note-354)

The Austin metropolitan area includes 27 [charter school](/wiki/Charter_school "Charter school") systems.[[351]](#cite_note-austin-chamber.org-355) [University of Texas Elementary School](/wiki/University_of_Texas_Elementary_School "University of Texas Elementary School") is in the city.

There are two state-operated schools for children with disabilities: [Texas School for the Blind and Visually Impaired](/wiki/Texas_School_for_the_Blind_and_Visually_Impaired "Texas School for the Blind and Visually Impaired") and [Texas School for the Deaf](/wiki/Texas_School_for_the_Deaf "Texas School for the Deaf"). [Texas Blind, Deaf, and Orphan School](/wiki/Texas_Blind,_Deaf,_and_Orphan_School "Texas Blind, Deaf, and Orphan School"), in the segregation era, had black deaf and blind students.

### Private education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=54 "Edit section: Private education")]

See also: [List of schools in the Roman Catholic Diocese of Austin](/wiki/List_of_schools_in_the_Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Austin "List of schools in the Roman Catholic Diocese of Austin")

The Austin metropolitan area includes over 100 private schools.[[351]](#cite_note-austin-chamber.org-355) Austin is also home to several child developmental institutions.

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin,_Texas&action=edit&section=55 "Edit section: Media")]

See also: [List of newspapers in Texas](/wiki/List_of_newspapers_in_Texas "List of newspapers in Texas"), [List of radio stations in Texas](/wiki/List_of_radio_stations_in_Texas "List of radio stations in Texas"), and [List of television stations in Texas](/wiki/List_of_television_stations_in_Texas "List of television stations in Texas")

Austin's main daily newspaper is the *[Austin American-Statesman](/wiki/Austin_American-Statesman "Austin American-Statesman")*. *[The Austin Chronicle](/wiki/The_Austin_Chronicle "The Austin Chronicle")* is Austin's [alternative weekly](/wiki/Alternative_weekly "Alternative weekly"), while *[The Daily Texan](/wiki/The_Daily_Texan "The Daily Texan")* is the student newspaper of the [University of Texas at Austin](/wiki/University_of_Texas_at_Austin "University of Texas at Austin"). Austin's business newspaper is the weekly *[Austin Business Journal](/wiki/Austin_Business_Journal "Austin Business Journal")*. *The Austin Monitor* is an online outlet that specializes in insider reporting on City Hall, Travis County Commissioners Court, [AISD](/wiki/Austin_Independent_School_District "Austin Independent School District"), and other related local civics beats. The *Monitor* is backed by the nonprofit Capital of Texas Media Foundation. Austin also has numerous smaller special interest or sub-regional newspapers such as the *[Oak Hill Gazette](/wiki/Oak_Hill_Gazette "Oak Hill Gazette")*, *Westlake Picayune*, *Hill Country News*, *Round Rock Leader*, *NOKOA*, and *The Villager* among others. *[Texas Monthly](/wiki/Texas_Monthly "Texas Monthly")*, a major regional magazine, is also headquartered in Austin. The *[Texas Observer](/wiki/Texas_Observer "Texas Observer")*, a muckraking biweekly political magazine, has been based in Austin for over five decades. The weekly *[Community Impact Newspaper](/wiki/Community_Impact_Newspaper "Community Impact Newspaper")* published by John Garrett, former publisher of the *[Austin Business Journal](/wiki/Austin_Business_Journal "Austin Business Journal")* has five regional editions and is delivered to every house and business within certain ZIP codes and all of the news is specific to those ZIP codes.[[352]](#cite_note-356) Another statewide publication based in Austin is *[The Texas Tribune](/wiki/The_Texas_Tribune "The Texas Tribune")*, an on-line publication focused on Texas politics.[[353]](#cite_note-nytimes.com-357) The *Tribune* is "user-supported" through donations, a business model similar to public radio.[[354]](#cite_note-358) The editor is [Evan Smith](/wiki/Evan_Smith_(journalist) "Evan Smith (journalist)"), former editor of *Texas Monthly*. Smith co-founded the *Texas Tribune*, a nonprofit, non-partisan public media organization, with Austin venture capitalist John Thornton and veteran journalist Ross Ramsey.[[353]](#cite_note-nytimes.com-357)[[355]](#cite_note-359)

Commercial radio stations include [KASE-FM](/wiki/KASE-FM "KASE-FM") ([country](/wiki/Country_music "Country music")), [KVET](/wiki/KVET_(AM) "KVET (AM)") (sports), [KVET-FM](/wiki/KVET-FM "KVET-FM") (country), [KKMJ-FM](/wiki/KKMJ-FM "KKMJ-FM") ([adult contemporary](/wiki/Adult_contemporary "Adult contemporary")), [KLBJ](/wiki/KLBJ_(AM) "KLBJ (AM)") (talk), [KLBJ-FM](/wiki/KLBJ-FM "KLBJ-FM") ([classic rock](/wiki/Classic_rock "Classic rock")), [KJFK](/wiki/KJFK_(AM) "KJFK (AM)") (variety hits), [KFMK](/wiki/KFMK "KFMK") (contemporary Christian), [KOKE-FM](/wiki/KOKE-FM "KOKE-FM") ([progressive country](/wiki/Progressive_country "Progressive country")) and [KPEZ](/wiki/KPEZ "KPEZ") ([rhythmic contemporary](/wiki/Rhythmic_contemporary "Rhythmic contemporary")). [KUT-FM](/wiki/KUT-FM "KUT-FM") is the leading [public radio](/wiki/Public_broadcasting "Public broadcasting") station in Texas and produces the majority of its content locally.[[356]](#cite_note-360) [KOOP (FM)](/wiki/KOOP_(FM) "KOOP (FM)") is a volunteer-run radio station with more than 60 locally produced programs.[[357]](#cite_note-361) [KVRX](/wiki/KVRX "KVRX") is the student-run college radio station of the University of Texas at Austin with a focus on local and non-mainstream music and community programming.[[358]](#cite_note-362) Other listener-supported stations include [KAZI](/wiki/KAZI "KAZI") ([urban contemporary](/wiki/Urban_contemporary "Urban contemporary")), and [KMFA](/wiki/KMFA "KMFA") ([classical](/wiki/Classical_music "Classical music")).

Network television stations (affiliations in parentheses) include [KTBC](/wiki/KTBC_(TV) "KTBC (TV)") (Fox [O&O](/wiki/Owned-and-operated_station "Owned-and-operated station")), [KVUE](/wiki/KVUE "KVUE") (ABC), [KXAN](/wiki/KXAN "KXAN") (NBC), [KEYE-TV](/wiki/KEYE-TV "KEYE-TV") (CBS), [KLRU](/wiki/KLRU "KLRU") (PBS), [KNVA](/wiki/KNVA "KNVA") (The CW), [KBVO](/wiki/KBVO_(TV) "KBVO (TV)") (MyNetworkTV), and [KAKW](/wiki/KAKW "KAKW") (Univision [O&O](/wiki/Owned-and-operated_station "Owned-and-operated station")). KLRU produces several award-winning locally produced programs such as *[Austin City Limits](/wiki/Austin_City_Limits "Austin City Limits")*.[[359]](#cite_note-363) Despite Austin's explosive growth, it is only a medium-sized market (currently 38th) because the suburban and rural areas are not much larger than the city proper. Additionally, the proximity of San Antonio truncates the potential market area.

Alex Jones, journalist, radio show host and filmmaker, produces his talk show *[The Alex Jones Show](/wiki/The_Alex_Jones_Show "The Alex Jones Show")* in Austin which broadcasts nationally on more than 60 AM and FM radio stations in the United States, [WWCR](/wiki/WWCR "WWCR") Radio shortwave and [XM Radio](/wiki/XM_Radio "XM Radio"): Channel 166.[[360]](#cite_note-364)
